([/tæzˈmeɪniə/](/wiki/Help:IPA/English "Help:IPA/English"); [palawa kani](/wiki/Palawa_kani "Palawa kani"): *Lutruwita*) is an island [state](/wiki/States_and_territories_of_Australia "States and territories of Australia") of [Australia](/wiki/Australia "Australia").[[13]](#cite_note-13) It is located 240 kilometres (150 miles) to the south of the [Australian mainland](/wiki/Mainland_Australia "Mainland Australia"), and is separated from it by the [Bass Strait](/wiki/Bass_Strait "Bass Strait"). The state encompasses the main island of Tasmania, the [26th-largest island in the world](/wiki/List_of_islands_by_area#Islands "List of islands by area"), and the [surrounding 1000 islands](/wiki/List_of_islands_of_Tasmania "List of islands of Tasmania").[[14]](#cite_note-14) It is Australia's smallest and least populous state, with 573,479 residents as of June 2023[[update]](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Tasmania&action=edit). The [state capital](/wiki/List_of_Australian_capital_cities "List of Australian capital cities") and largest city is [Hobart](/wiki/Hobart "Hobart"), with around 40% of the population living in the Greater Hobart area.[[15]](#cite_note-ABSCapitalPop2-15) Tasmania is the most decentralised state in Australia, with the lowest proportion of its residents living within its capital city.[[16]](#cite_note-16)

Tasmania from space

**Tasmania** ([/tæzˈmeɪniə/](/wiki/Help:IPA/English "Help:IPA/English"); [palawa kani](/wiki/Palawa_kani "Palawa kani"): *Lutruwita*) is an island [state](/wiki/States_and_territories_of_Australia "States and territories of Australia") of [Australia](/wiki/Australia "Australia").[[13]](#cite_note-13) It is located 240 kilometres (150 miles) to the south of the [Australian mainland](/wiki/Mainland_Australia "Mainland Australia"), and is separated from it by the [Bass Strait](/wiki/Bass_Strait "Bass Strait"). The state encompasses the main island of Tasmania, the [26th-largest island in the world](/wiki/List_of_islands_by_area#Islands "List of islands by area"), and the [surrounding 1000 islands](/wiki/List_of_islands_of_Tasmania "List of islands of Tasmania").[[14]](#cite_note-14) It is Australia's smallest and least populous state, with 573,479 residents as of June 2023[[update]](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Tasmania&action=edit). The [state capital](/wiki/List_of_Australian_capital_cities "List of Australian capital cities") and largest city is [Hobart](/wiki/Hobart "Hobart"), with around 40% of the population living in the Greater Hobart area.[[15]](#cite_note-ABSCapitalPop2-15) Tasmania is the most decentralised state in Australia, with the lowest proportion of its residents living within its capital city.[[16]](#cite_note-16)

Tasmania's main island was first inhabited by [Aboriginal peoples](/wiki/Aboriginal_Australians "Aboriginal Australians"), who today generally identify as Palawa or Pakana.[[17]](#cite_note-17)[[18]](#cite_note-18)[[19]](#cite_note-19)
It is believed that [Aboriginal Tasmanians](/wiki/Aboriginal_Tasmanians "Aboriginal Tasmanians") became isolated from mainland Aboriginal groups around 11,700 years ago, when rising sea levels formed [Bass Strait](/wiki/Bass_Strait "Bass Strait").[[20]](#cite_note-nma2-20)

In 1803, Tasmania was permanently settled by Europeans as a [penal settlement](/wiki/Penal_colony "Penal colony") of the [British Empire](/wiki/British_Empire "British Empire"), partly to pre-empt French territorial claims during the [Napoleonic Wars](/wiki/Napoleonic_Wars "Napoleonic Wars").[[21]](#cite_note-21) At the time of British arrival, the Aboriginal population is estimated to have been between 3,000 and 7,000. Within three decades, however, this number declined drastically due to violent conflict, known as the [Black War](/wiki/Black_War "Black War"), and the spread of [infectious diseases](/wiki/Infectious_disease "Infectious disease"). The Black War peaked between 1825 and 1831, resulting in the declaration of martial law for more than three years and causing the deaths of nearly 1,100 Aboriginal people and settlers.

Under British rule, the island was initially part of the [Colony of New South Wales](/wiki/Colony_of_New_South_Wales "Colony of New South Wales"); however, it became a separate colony under the name [Van Diemen's Land](/wiki/Van_Diemen%27s_Land "Van Diemen's Land") (named after [Anthony van Diemen](/wiki/Anthony_van_Diemen "Anthony van Diemen")) in 1825.[[22]](#cite_note-22) Approximately 80,000 [convicts](/wiki/Convicts_in_Australia "Convicts in Australia") were sent to Van Diemen's Land before this practice, known as [transportation](/wiki/Penal_transportation "Penal transportation"), ceased in 1853.[[23]](#cite_note-23) In 1855, the present Constitution of Tasmania was enacted, and the following year the colony formally changed its name to Tasmania. In 1901, it became a founding constituent state of Australia (one of six) through the process of the [federation of Australia](/wiki/Federation_of_Australia "Federation of Australia").

Today, Tasmania has the [second smallest economy of the Australian states and territories](/wiki/List_of_Australian_states_and_territories_by_gross_state_product "List of Australian states and territories by gross state product"); economic activity involves principally tourism, agriculture, aquaculture, education, and healthcare.[[24]](#cite_note-24) Tasmania is a significant agricultural exporter, as well as a significant destination for eco-tourism. About 42% of its land area, including [national parks](/wiki/National_parks "National parks") and [World Heritage Sites](/wiki/Tasmanian_Wilderness "Tasmanian Wilderness") (21%), is protected in some form of reserve.[[25]](#cite_note-25) The [first environmental political party](/wiki/United_Tasmania_Group "United Tasmania Group") in the world was founded in Tasmania.[[26]](#cite_note-26)

## Toponymy

Tasmania is named after [Dutch](/wiki/Dutch_people "Dutch people") [explorer](/wiki/Explorer "Explorer") [Abel Tasman](/wiki/Abel_Tasman "Abel Tasman"), who made the first reported European sighting of the island on 24 November 1642. Tasman named the island **Anthony van Diemen's Land** after his sponsor [Anthony van Diemen](/wiki/Anthony_van_Diemen "Anthony van Diemen"), the Governor of the [Dutch East Indies](/wiki/Dutch_East_Indies "Dutch East Indies"). The name was later shortened to **Van Diemen's Land** by the British. It was officially renamed "Tasmania" in honour of its first European discoverer on 1 January 1856.[[27]](#cite_note-27)

Tasmania was sometimes referred to as "Dervon", as mentioned in the [Jerilderie Letter](/wiki/Jerilderie_Letter "Jerilderie Letter") written by the notorious Australian [bushranger](/wiki/Bushranger "Bushranger") [Ned Kelly](/wiki/Ned_Kelly "Ned Kelly") in 1879. The colloquial expression or nickname for the state is "Tassie". Tasmania is also sometimes referenced by its short-form name "Tas", mainly when written (eg, in business names, websites, addresses). TAS is also the [Australia Post abbreviation](/wiki/Australian_postal_abbreviations "Australian postal abbreviations") for the state.

### Names from Indigenous languages

In the constructed [palawa kani](/wiki/Palawa_kani "Palawa kani") language, the main island of Tasmania is called "Lutruwita",[[28]](#cite_note-:4-28) a name originally derived from the [Bruny Island Tasmanian language](/wiki/Bruny_Island_Tasmanian_language "Bruny Island Tasmanian language"). [George Augustus Robinson](/wiki/George_Augustus_Robinson "George Augustus Robinson") recorded it as *Loe.trou.witter*, but also as *Trow.wer.nar*, probably from one or more of the [eastern](/wiki/Eastern_Tasmanian_languages "Eastern Tasmanian languages") or [Northeastern Tasmanian languages](/wiki/Northeastern_Tasmanian_languages "Northeastern Tasmanian languages"). However, he also recorded this name for [Cape Barren Island](/wiki/Cape_Barren_Island "Cape Barren Island"). In the 20th century, some writers used it as an Aboriginal name for Tasmania—spelled "Trowenna" or "Trowunna". It is now believed that that name is more properly applied to Cape Barren Island,[[28]](#cite_note-:4-28) which has had an official [dual name](/wiki/Dual_naming "Dual naming") of "Truwana" since 2014.[[29]](#cite_note-official-aboriginal-29)

A number of [palawa kani](/wiki/Palawa_kani "Palawa kani") names, based on historical records of aboriginal names, have been accepted by the Tasmanian government. A dozen of these (below) are 'dual-use' (bilingual) names, and another two are unbounded areas with only palawa names.[[29]](#cite_note-official-aboriginal-29)

Bilingual names

* Kanamaluka / [Tamar River](/wiki/Tamar_River "Tamar River")
* Kunanyi / [Mount Wellington](/wiki/Mount_Wellington_(Tasmania) "Mount Wellington (Tasmania)")
* Laraturunawn / Sundown Point
* Nungu / West Point
* Pinmatik / [Rocky Cape](/wiki/Rocky_Cape,_Tasmania "Rocky Cape, Tasmania")
* Takayna / [The Tarkine](/wiki/The_Tarkine "The Tarkine")
* Taypalaka / [Green Point](/wiki/Green_Point,_Tasmania "Green Point, Tasmania")
* Titima / [Trefoil Island](/wiki/Trefoil_Island "Trefoil Island")
* Truwana / [Cape Barren Island](/wiki/Cape_Barren_Island "Cape Barren Island")
* Wukalina / [Mount William](/wiki/Mount_William_National_Park "Mount William National Park")
* Yingina / [Great Lake](/wiki/Great_Lake_(Tasmania) "Great Lake (Tasmania)")

Palawa names

* Larapuna: an unbounded area centred on the [Bay of Fires](/wiki/Bay_of_Fires "Bay of Fires")
* [Narawntapu National Park](/wiki/Narawntapu_National_Park "Narawntapu National Park") (formerly Asbestos Range National Park)
* Putalina: an unbounded area centred on Oyster Cove (including the community of [Oyster Cove](/wiki/Oyster_Cove,_Tasmania "Oyster Cove, Tasmania"))

There are also a number of archaeological sites with Palawa names. Some of these names have been contentious, with names being proposed without consultation with the aboriginal community, or without having a connection to the place in question.[[30]](#cite_note-30)

As well as a diverse First Nations geography, where remnants are preserved in rough form by European documentation, Tasmania is known as a place for [unorthodox place-names](/wiki/Place_names_considered_unusual "Place names considered unusual").[[31]](#cite_note-31) These names often come about from lost definitions, where descriptive names have lost their old meanings and have taken on new modern interpretations (e.g. 'Bobs Knobs'). Other names have retained their original meaning, and are often quaint or endearing descriptions (e.g. ['Paradise'](/wiki/Paradise,_Tasmania "Paradise, Tasmania")).

## History

Main article: [History of Tasmania](/wiki/History_of_Tasmania "History of Tasmania")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/63/Tasmania_simple_geology_map.png/250px-Tasmania_simple_geology_map.png)](/wiki/File:Tasmania_simple_geology_map.png)

Map showing the general geological surface features of Tasmania. Note the extent of [dolerite](/wiki/Diabase "Diabase"), and the mosaics in the west.

### Physical history

Main article: [Geology of Tasmania](/wiki/Geology_of_Tasmania "Geology of Tasmania")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/9f/Tessellated_Pavement_Sunrise_Landscape.jpg/250px-Tessellated_Pavement_Sunrise_Landscape.jpg)](/wiki/File:Tessellated_Pavement_Sunrise_Landscape.jpg)

[Tessellated pavement](/wiki/Tessellated_pavement "Tessellated pavement"), a rare rock formation on the [Tasman Peninsula](/wiki/Tasman_Peninsula "Tasman Peninsula")

The island was adjoined to the mainland of Australia until the end of the [last glacial period](/wiki/Last_glacial_period "Last glacial period") about 11,700 years ago.[[20]](#cite_note-nma2-20) Much of the island is composed of [Jurassic](/wiki/Jurassic "Jurassic") [dolerite](/wiki/Diabase "Diabase") intrusions (the upwelling of [magma](/wiki/Magma "Magma")) through other rock types, sometimes forming large columnar joints. Tasmania has the world's largest areas of dolerite, with many distinctive mountains and cliffs formed from this rock type. The [central plateau](/wiki/Central_Plateau_(Tasmania) "Central Plateau (Tasmania)") and the southeast portions of the island are mostly dolerites. [Mount Wellington](/wiki/Mount_Wellington_(Tasmania) "Mount Wellington (Tasmania)") above [Hobart](/wiki/Hobart "Hobart") is a good example, showing distinct columns known as the Organ Pipes.

In the southern midlands as far south as Hobart, the dolerite is underlaid by [sandstone](/wiki/Sandstone "Sandstone") and similar sedimentary stones. In the southwest, [Precambrian](/wiki/Precambrian "Precambrian") [quartzites](/wiki/Quartzite "Quartzite") were formed from very ancient sea sediments and form strikingly sharp ridges and ranges, such as Federation Peak or [Frenchmans Cap](/wiki/Frenchmans_Cap "Frenchmans Cap").

In the northeast and east, continental [granites](/wiki/Granite "Granite") can be seen, such as at Freycinet, similar to coastal granites on mainland Australia. In the northwest and west, mineral-rich volcanic rock can be seen at [Mount Read](/wiki/Mount_Read_(Tasmania) "Mount Read (Tasmania)") near [Rosebery](/wiki/Rosebery,_Tasmania "Rosebery, Tasmania"), or at [Mount Lyell](/wiki/Mount_Lyell,_Tasmania "Mount Lyell, Tasmania") near [Queenstown](/wiki/Queenstown,_Tasmania "Queenstown, Tasmania"). Also present in the south and northwest is [limestone](/wiki/Limestone "Limestone") with caves.

The quartzite and dolerite areas in the higher mountains show evidence of [glaciation](/wiki/Glaciation "Glaciation"), and much of Australia's glaciated landscape is found on the Central Plateau and the Southwest. [Cradle Mountain](/wiki/Cradle_Mountain "Cradle Mountain"), another dolerite peak, for example, was a [nunatak](/wiki/Nunatak "Nunatak"). The combination of these different rock types contributes to scenery which is distinct from any other region of the world.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] In the far southwest corner of the state, the geology is almost wholly quartzite, which gives the mountains the false impression of having snow-capped peaks year round.

### Aboriginal people

Main article: [Aboriginal Tasmanians](/wiki/Aboriginal_Tasmanians "Aboriginal Tasmanians")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/8b/Terre_de_Diemen%2C_navigation%2C_vue_de_la_cote_orientale_de_l%27Ile_Schouten.jpg/250px-Terre_de_Diemen%2C_navigation%2C_vue_de_la_cote_orientale_de_l%27Ile_Schouten.jpg)](/wiki/File:Terre_de_Diemen,_navigation,_vue_de_la_cote_orientale_de_l%27Ile_Schouten.jpg)

1807 engraving by French explorer [Charles Alexandre Lesueur](/wiki/Charles_Alexandre_Lesueur "Charles Alexandre Lesueur") shows seafaring Aboriginal people and a large canoe on the eastern shore of [Schouten Island](/wiki/Schouten_Island "Schouten Island").

Evidence indicates the presence of [Aboriginal people](/wiki/Aboriginal_Australians "Aboriginal Australians") in Tasmania about 42,000 years ago. [Rising sea levels](/wiki/Sea_level_rise "Sea level rise") cut Tasmania off from mainland Australia about 10,000 years ago and by the time of European contact, the Aboriginal people in Tasmania had nine major nations or ethnic groups.[[32]](#cite_note-ryan1-32) At the time of the British occupation and colonisation in 1803, the indigenous population was estimated at between 3,000 and 10,000.

Historian [Lyndall Ryan's](/wiki/Lyndall_Ryan "Lyndall Ryan") analysis of population studies led her to conclude that there were about 7,000 spread throughout the island's nine nations;[[33]](#cite_note-lyndall-33) Nicholas Clements, citing research by [N.J.B. Plomley](/wiki/Brian_Plomley "Brian Plomley") and [Rhys Jones](/wiki/Rhys_Jones_(archaeologist) "Rhys Jones (archaeologist)"), settled on a figure of 3,000 to 4,000.[[34]](#cite_note-34) They engaged in [fire-stick farming](/wiki/Fire-stick_farming "Fire-stick farming"), hunted game including [kangaroo](/wiki/Kangaroo "Kangaroo") and [wallabies](/wiki/Wallaby "Wallaby"), caught seals, mutton-birds, shellfish and fish and lived as nine separate "nations" on the island, which they knew as "Trouwunna".

### European arrival and governance

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/f0/Abel_Tasman_Navigateur_en_Australie_%28cropped%29.jpg/250px-Abel_Tasman_Navigateur_en_Australie_%28cropped%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Abel_Tasman_Navigateur_en_Australie_(cropped).jpg)

Tasmania is named after Dutch explorer [Abel Tasman](/wiki/Abel_Tasman "Abel Tasman"), the first European to sight the island, in 1642.

The first reported sighting of Tasmania by a [European](/wiki/Ethnic_groups_in_Europe "Ethnic groups in Europe") was on 24 November 1642 by Dutch explorer [Abel Tasman](/wiki/Abel_Tasman "Abel Tasman"), who landed at today's [Blackman Bay](/wiki/Blackman_Bay,_Tasmania "Blackman Bay, Tasmania"). More than a century later, in 1772, a French expedition led by [Marc-Joseph Marion du Fresne](/wiki/Marc-Joseph_Marion_du_Fresne "Marc-Joseph Marion du Fresne") landed at (nearby but different) [Blackmans Bay](/wiki/Blackmans_Bay,_Tasmania "Blackmans Bay, Tasmania"), and the following year [Tobias Furneaux](/wiki/Tobias_Furneaux "Tobias Furneaux") became the first Englishman to land in Tasmania when he arrived at [Adventure Bay](/wiki/Adventure_Bay,_Tasmania "Adventure Bay, Tasmania"), which he named after his ship [HMS *Adventure*](/wiki/HMS_Adventure_(1771) "HMS Adventure (1771)"). Captain [James Cook](/wiki/James_Cook "James Cook") also landed at [Adventure Bay](/wiki/Adventure_Bay,_Tasmania "Adventure Bay, Tasmania") in 1777. [Matthew Flinders](/wiki/Matthew_Flinders "Matthew Flinders") and [George Bass](/wiki/George_Bass "George Bass") sailed through [Bass Strait](/wiki/Bass_Strait "Bass Strait") in 1798–1799, determining for the first time that Tasmania was an island.[[35]](#cite_note-hughes121-35)

Sealers and whalers based themselves on Tasmania's islands from 1798,[[36]](#cite_note-36) and in August 1803 [New South Wales](/wiki/New_South_Wales "New South Wales") Governor [Philip King](/wiki/Philip_Gidley_King "Philip Gidley King") sent Lieutenant [John Bowen](/wiki/John_Bowen_(Royal_Navy_officer) "John Bowen (Royal Navy officer)") to establish a small military outpost on the eastern shore of the [Derwent River](/wiki/River_Derwent_(Tasmania) "River Derwent (Tasmania)") in order to forestall any claims to the island by French explorers who had been exploring the southern Australian coastline. Bowen, who led a party of 49, including 21 male and three female convicts, named the camp Risdon.[[35]](#cite_note-hughes121-35)[[37]](#cite_note-37)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/46/John_Glover_-_Mount_Wellington_and_Hobart_Town_from_Kangaroo_Point_-_Google_Art_Project.jpg/250px-John_Glover_-_Mount_Wellington_and_Hobart_Town_from_Kangaroo_Point_-_Google_Art_Project.jpg)](/wiki/File:John_Glover_-_Mount_Wellington_and_Hobart_Town_from_Kangaroo_Point_-_Google_Art_Project.jpg)

Painting by [John Glover](/wiki/John_Glover_(artist) "John Glover (artist)") of [Mount Wellington](/wiki/Mount_Wellington_(Tasmania) "Mount Wellington (Tasmania)") and [Hobart](/wiki/Hobart "Hobart"), 1834

Several months later, a second settlement was established by Captain [David Collins](/wiki/David_Collins_(lieutenant_governor) "David Collins (lieutenant governor)"), with 308 convicts, 5 kilometres (3.1 miles) to the south in [Sullivans Cove](/wiki/Sullivans_Cove "Sullivans Cove") on the western side of the Derwent, where fresh water was more plentiful. The latter settlement became known as Hobart Town or Hobarton, later shortened to [Hobart](/wiki/Hobart "Hobart"), after the [British Colonial Secretary](/wiki/Secretary_of_State_for_the_Colonies "Secretary of State for the Colonies") of the time, [Lord Hobart](/wiki/Lord_Hobart "Lord Hobart"). The settlement at Risdon was later abandoned. Left on their own without further supplies, the Sullivans Cove settlement suffered severe food shortages and by 1806 its inhabitants were starving, with many resorting to scraping seaweed off rocks and scavenging washed-up whale blubber from the shore to survive.[[35]](#cite_note-hughes121-35)

A smaller colony was established at Port Dalrymple on the Tamar River in the island's north in October 1804 and several other convict-based settlements were established, including the particularly harsh [penal colonies](/wiki/Penal_colony "Penal colony") at [Port Arthur](/wiki/Port_Arthur,_Tasmania "Port Arthur, Tasmania") in the southeast and [Macquarie Harbour](/wiki/Macquarie_Harbour "Macquarie Harbour") on the West Coast. Tasmania was eventually sent 75,000 convicts—four out of every ten people transported to Australia.[[35]](#cite_note-hughes121-35) By 1819, the Aboriginal and British population reached parity with about 5000 of each, although among the colonists men outnumbered women four-to-one.[[38]](#cite_note-parity-38) Free settlers began arriving in large numbers from 1820, lured by the promise of land grants and free convict labour. Settlement in the island's northwest corner was monopolised by the [Van Diemen's Land Company](/wiki/Van_Diemen%27s_Land_Company "Van Diemen's Land Company"), which sent its first surveyors to the district in 1826. By 1830, one-third of Australia's non-Indigenous population lived in Van Diemen's Land and the island accounted for about half of all land under cultivation and exports.[[39]](#cite_note-39)

#### Black War

Main article: [Black War](/wiki/Black_War "Black War")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/9d/Benjamin_Duterrau_-_Timmy%2C_a_Tasmanian_Aboriginal%2C_throwing_a_spear_-_Google_Art_Project.jpg/250px-Benjamin_Duterrau_-_Timmy%2C_a_Tasmanian_Aboriginal%2C_throwing_a_spear_-_Google_Art_Project.jpg)](/wiki/File:Benjamin_Duterrau_-_Timmy,_a_Tasmanian_Aboriginal,_throwing_a_spear_-_Google_Art_Project.jpg)

Painting of a Tasmanian Aboriginal throwing a spear, 1838

Tensions between Tasmania's Aboriginal and white inhabitants rose, partly driven by increasing competition for kangaroo and other game.[[40]](#cite_note-40)[[41]](#cite_note-41)[[42]](#cite_note-42) Explorer and naval officer [John Oxley](/wiki/John_Oxley "John Oxley") in 1810 noted the "many atrocious cruelties" inflicted on Aboriginal people by convict [bushrangers](/wiki/Bushranger "Bushranger") in the north, which in turn led to black attacks on solitary white hunters.[[43]](#cite_note-43) Hostilities increased further with the arrival of 600 colonists from [Norfolk Island](/wiki/Norfolk_Island "Norfolk Island") between 1807 and 1813. They established farms along the River Derwent and east and west of [Launceston](/wiki/Launceston,_Tasmania "Launceston, Tasmania"), occupying ten percent of Van Diemen's Land. By 1824 the colonial population had swelled to 12,600, while the island's sheep population had reached 200,000. The rapid colonisation transformed traditional kangaroo hunting grounds into farms with grazing livestock as well as fences, hedges and stone walls, while police and military patrols were increased to control the convict farm labourers.[[44]](#cite_note-44)

Violence began to spiral rapidly from the mid-1820s in what became described as the "[Black War](/wiki/Black_War "Black War")".[[45]](#cite_note-45) Aboriginal inhabitants were driven to desperation by hunger – that included a desire for agricultural produce, as well as feeling anger at the prevalence of abductions of women and girls. New settlers motivated by fear carried out self-defence operations as well as attacks as a means of suppressing the native threat – or even in some cases, exacting revenge.[[46]](#cite_note-46) Van Diemen's Land had an enormous gender imbalance, with male colonists outnumbering females six to one in 1822—and 16 to one among the convict population. Historian Nicholas Clements has suggested the "voracious appetite" for native women was the most important trigger for the explosion of violence from the late 1820s.[[47]](#cite_note-47)

From 1825 to 1828, the number of native attacks more than doubled each year, raising panic among settlers. Over the summer of 1826–1827 clans from the Big River, Oyster Bay and North Midlands nations speared stock-keepers on farms and made it clear that they wanted the settlers and their sheep and cattle to move from their kangaroo hunting grounds. Settlers responded vigorously, resulting in many mass-killings. In November 1826, Governor [Sir George Arthur](/wiki/Sir_George_Arthur,_1st_Baronet "Sir George Arthur, 1st Baronet") issued a government notice declaring that colonists were free to kill Aboriginal people when they attacked settlers or their property, and in the following eight months more than 200 Aboriginal people were killed in the Settled Districts in reprisal for the deaths of 15 colonists. After another eight months, the death toll had risen to 43 colonists and probably 350 Aboriginal people.[[48]](#cite_note-48) In April 1828, Arthur issued a [Proclamation of Demarcation](/wiki/1828_Proclamation_of_Demarcation "1828 Proclamation of Demarcation") forbidding Aboriginal people to enter the settled districts without a passport issued by the government.[[49]](#cite_note-Carroll2014-49)[[50]](#cite_note-Morgan2003-50) Arthur declared [martial law](/wiki/Martial_law "Martial law") in the colony in November that year, and this remained in force for over three years, the longest period of martial law in Australian history.[[51]](#cite_note-51)[[52]](#cite_note-52)

In November 1830, Arthur organised the so-called "[Black Line](/wiki/Black_Line "Black Line")", ordering every able-bodied male colonist to assemble at one of seven designated places in the Settled Districts to join a massive drive to sweep Aboriginal people out of the region and on to the [Tasman Peninsula](/wiki/Tasman_Peninsula "Tasman Peninsula"). The campaign failed and was abandoned seven weeks later, but by then Tasmania's Aboriginal population had fallen to about 300.[[53]](#cite_note-53)

#### Removal of Aboriginal people

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/41/Truganini_and_last_4_tasmanian_aborigines.jpg/250px-Truganini_and_last_4_tasmanian_aborigines.jpg)](/wiki/File:Truganini_and_last_4_tasmanian_aborigines.jpg)

Four elderly full-blood Tasmanian Aboriginal people, c. 1860s. [Truganini](/wiki/Truganini "Truganini"), for many years claimed to be the last full-blood Aboriginal person to survive, is seated far right.

After hostilities between settlers and Aboriginal peoples ceased in 1832, almost all of the remnants of the Indigenous population were persuaded by government agent [George Augustus Robinson](/wiki/George_Augustus_Robinson "George Augustus Robinson") to move to [Flinders Island](/wiki/Flinders_Island,_Tasmania "Flinders Island, Tasmania"). Many quickly succumbed to infectious diseases to which they had no immunity, reducing the population further.[[54]](#cite_note-54)[[55]](#cite_note-55) Of those removed from Tasmania, the last to die was [Truganini](/wiki/Truganini "Truganini"), in 1876.

The near-destruction of Tasmania's Aboriginal population has been described as an act of genocide by historians including [Robert Hughes](/wiki/Robert_Hughes_(critic) "Robert Hughes (critic)"), [James Boyce](/wiki/James_Boyce_(author) "James Boyce (author)"), [Lyndall Ryan](/wiki/Lyndall_Ryan "Lyndall Ryan") and Tom Lawson.[[35]](#cite_note-hughes121-35)[[56]](#cite_note-56)[[57]](#cite_note-57) However, other historians including [Henry Reynolds](/wiki/Henry_Reynolds_(historian) "Henry Reynolds (historian)"), [Richard Broome](/wiki/Richard_Broome "Richard Broome") and Nicholas Clements do not agree with the genocide thesis, arguing that the colonial authorities did not intend to destroy the Aboriginal population in whole or in part.[[58]](#cite_note-:1-58)[[59]](#cite_note-:2-59) Boyce has claimed that the April 1828 "Proclamation Separating the Aborigines from the White Inhabitants" sanctioned force against Aboriginal people "for no other reason than that they were Aboriginal".[[60]](#cite_note-:3-60) However, as Reynolds, Broome and Clements point out, there was open warfare at the time.[[58]](#cite_note-:1-58)[[59]](#cite_note-:2-59) Boyce described the decision to remove all Tasmanian Aboriginal people after 1832—by which time they had given up their fight against white colonists—as an extreme policy position. He concluded: "The colonial government from 1832 to 1838 [ethnically cleansed](/wiki/Ethnic_cleansing "Ethnic cleansing") the western half of Van Diemen's Land."[[60]](#cite_note-:3-60) Nevertheless, Clements and Flood note that there was another wave of violence in north-west Tasmania in 1841, involving attacks on settlers' huts by a band of Aboriginal Tasmanians who had not been removed from the island.[[61]](#cite_note-61)[[62]](#cite_note-62)

#### Proclamation as a colony

Further information: [Colony of Tasmania](/wiki/Colony_of_Tasmania "Colony of Tasmania")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/af/Convict_labourers_in_Australia_in_the_early_20th_century.jpg/250px-Convict_labourers_in_Australia_in_the_early_20th_century.jpg)](/wiki/File:Convict_labourers_in_Australia_in_the_early_20th_century.jpg)

A [convict](/wiki/Convicts_in_Australia "Convicts in Australia") ploughing team breaking up new ground at the farm at Port Arthur

Van Diemen's Land—which thus far had existed as a territory within the colony of [New South Wales](/wiki/New_South_Wales "New South Wales")—was proclaimed a separate colony, with its own judicial establishment and [Legislative Council](/wiki/Tasmanian_Legislative_Council "Tasmanian Legislative Council"), on 3 December 1825. Transportation to the island ceased in 1853 and the colony was renamed Tasmania in 1856, partly to differentiate the burgeoning society of free settlers from the island's convict past.[[63]](#cite_note-63)

The [Legislative Council of Van Diemen's Land](/wiki/Tasmanian_Legislative_Council "Tasmanian Legislative Council") drafted a new constitution which gained Royal Assent in 1855. The [Privy Council](/wiki/Privy_Council "Privy Council") also approved the colony changing its name from "Van Diemen's Land" to "Tasmania", and in 1856 the newly elected [bicameral parliament](/wiki/Bicameral_parliament "Bicameral parliament") sat for the first time, establishing Tasmania as a [self-governing](/wiki/Responsible_government "Responsible government") colony of the British Empire.[[64]](#cite_note-64)

The colony suffered from economic fluctuations, but for the most part was prosperous, experiencing steady growth. With few external threats and strong trade links with the Empire, Tasmania enjoyed many fruitful periods in the late 19th century, becoming a world-centre of shipbuilding. It raised a local defence force that eventually played a [significant role](/wiki/Military_history_of_Australia_during_the_Second_Boer_War "Military history of Australia during the Second Boer War") in the [Second Boer War](/wiki/Second_Boer_War "Second Boer War") in South Africa, and Tasmanian soldiers in that conflict won the first two [Victoria Crosses](/wiki/Victoria_Cross "Victoria Cross") awarded to Australians.

#### Federation

In 1901, the Colony of Tasmania [united](/wiki/Federation_of_Australia "Federation of Australia") with the five other Australian colonies to form the Commonwealth of Australia. Tasmanians voted in favour of federation with the largest majority of all the Australian colonies.

### 20th and 21st century

Tasmania was an early adopter of [electric](/wiki/Electric "Electric") [street lighting](/wiki/Street_lighting "Street lighting"). Australia's first electric street lights were switched on in [Waratah](/wiki/Waratah,_Tasmania "Waratah, Tasmania") in 1886.[[65]](#cite_note-65) [Launceston](/wiki/Launceston,_Tasmania "Launceston, Tasmania") became the first completely electrified city on the island in 1885, followed closely by the township of [Zeehan](/wiki/Zeehan "Zeehan") in 1900.

The state economy was riding mining prosperity until World War I. In 1901, the state population was 172,475.[[66]](#cite_note-66) The 1910 foundation of what would become [Hydro Tasmania](/wiki/Hydro_Tasmania "Hydro Tasmania") began to shape urban patterns, as well as future major damming programs.[[67]](#cite_note-urbanisation_cultural_artefact-67) Hydro's influence culminated in the 1970s when the state government announced plans to flood environmentally significant [Lake Pedder](/wiki/Lake_Pedder "Lake Pedder"). As a result of the eventual flooding of Lake Pedder, the world's first green party was established; the [United Tasmania Group](/wiki/United_Tasmania_Group "United Tasmania Group").[[68]](#cite_note-68) National and international attention surrounded the campaign against the [Franklin Dam](/wiki/Franklin_Dam "Franklin Dam") in the early 1980s.[[69]](#cite_note-69)

Tasmanian [Enid Lyons](/wiki/Enid_Lyons "Enid Lyons") became the first female member of the [House of Representatives](/wiki/Australian_House_of_Representatives "Australian House of Representatives") at the [1943 federal election](/wiki/1943_Australian_federal_election "1943 Australian federal election") and first female to serve in the [federal cabinet](/wiki/Cabinet_of_Australia "Cabinet of Australia"). In May 1948, [Margaret McIntyre](/wiki/Margaret_McIntyre "Margaret McIntyre") achieved another milestone as the first female elected to the Parliament of Tasmania. Less than six months after her election, McIntyre died in the [crash of the *Lutana*](/wiki/1948_Australian_National_Airways_DC-3_crash "1948 Australian National Airways DC-3 crash") near [Quirindi](/wiki/Quirindi,_New_South_Wales "Quirindi, New South Wales") on 2 September 1948.

After the end of World War II, the state saw major urbanisation, and the growth of towns like [Ulverstone](/wiki/Ulverstone,_Tasmania "Ulverstone, Tasmania").[[67]](#cite_note-urbanisation_cultural_artefact-67) It gained a reputation as "Sanitorium of the South" and a health-focused tourist boom began to grow. The *[Princess of Tasmania](/wiki/Princess_of_Tasmania "Princess of Tasmania")* began her maiden voyage in 1959, the first car ferry to Tasmania.[[67]](#cite_note-urbanisation_cultural_artefact-67) As part of the boom, Tasmania allowed the opening of the first casino in Australia in 1968.[[67]](#cite_note-urbanisation_cultural_artefact-67) Queen [Elizabeth II](/wiki/Elizabeth_II "Elizabeth II") visited the state in 1954, and the 50s and 60s were charactered by the opening of major public services, including the Tasmanian Housing Department and [Metro Tasmania](/wiki/Metro_Tasmania "Metro Tasmania") public bus services. A jail was opened at Risdon in 1960, and the [State Library of Tasmania](/wiki/State_Library_of_Tasmania "State Library of Tasmania") the same year. The University of Tasmania also moved to its present location in 1963.

The state was badly affected by the [1967 Tasmanian fires](/wiki/1967_Tasmanian_fires "1967 Tasmanian fires"), killing 64 people and destroying over 652,000 acres in five hours.[[70]](#cite_note-70) In 1975 the [Tasman Bridge collapsed](/wiki/Tasman_Bridge_disaster "Tasman Bridge disaster") when the bridge was struck by the bulk ore carrier *[Lake Illawarra](/wiki/SS_Lake_Illawarra "SS Lake Illawarra")*. It was the only bridge in Hobart, and made crossing the [Derwent River](/wiki/Derwent_River_(Tasmania) "Derwent River (Tasmania)") by road at the city impossible. The nearest bridge was approximately 20 kilometres (12 mi) to the north, at Bridgewater.

Throughout the 1980s, strong environmental concerns saw the building of the Australian Antarctic Division headquarters, and the proclamation of the [Tasmanian Wilderness World Heritage Area](/wiki/Tasmanian_Wilderness_World_Heritage_Area "Tasmanian Wilderness World Heritage Area"). The Franklin Dam was blocked by the federal government in 1983, and [CSIRO](/wiki/CSIRO "CSIRO") opened its marine studies centre in Hobart. [Pope John Paul II](/wiki/Pope_John_Paul_II "Pope John Paul II") would hold mass at [Elwick Racecourse](/wiki/Elwick_Racecourse "Elwick Racecourse") in 1986.

The 1990s were characterised by the fight for [LGBT rights in Tasmania](/wiki/LGBT_rights_in_Tasmania "LGBT rights in Tasmania"), culminating in the intervention of the [United Nations Human Rights Committee](/wiki/United_Nations_Human_Rights_Committee "United Nations Human Rights Committee") in 1997 and the decriminalization of homosexuality that year.[[71]](#cite_note-71) [Christine Milne](/wiki/Christine_Milne "Christine Milne") became the first female leader of a Tasmanian political party in 1993, and major council amalgamations reduce the number of councils from 46 to 29.

Following the [Port Arthur massacre](/wiki/Port_Arthur_massacre "Port Arthur massacre") on 28 April 1996, which resulted in the loss of 35 lives and injured 23 others, the Australian Government conducted a review of its [firearms](/wiki/Firearm "Firearm") policies and enacted new [nationwide gun ownership laws](/wiki/Gun_laws_of_Australia "Gun laws of Australia") under the [National Firearms Agreement](/wiki/National_Firearms_Agreement "National Firearms Agreement").

In 2000, Queen Elizabeth II once again visited the state. [Gunns](/wiki/Gunns "Gunns") rose to prominence as a major forestry company during this decade, only to collapse in 2013. In 2004, Premier [Jim Bacon](/wiki/Jim_Bacon_(politician) "Jim Bacon (politician)") died in office from lung cancer. In January 2011 philanthropist [David Walsh](/wiki/David_Walsh_(art_collector) "David Walsh (art collector)") opened the [Museum of Old and New Art](/wiki/Museum_of_Old_and_New_Art "Museum of Old and New Art") (MONA) in Hobart to international acclaim. Within 12 months, MONA became Tasmania's top tourism attraction.[[72]](#cite_note-72)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/f2/Port_Arthur_Panorama.jpg/960px-Port_Arthur_Panorama.jpg)](/wiki/File:Port_Arthur_Panorama.jpg "Port Arthur, declared a World Heritage Site in 2010")

[Port Arthur](/wiki/Port_Arthur,_Tasmania "Port Arthur, Tasmania"), declared a [World Heritage Site](/wiki/World_Heritage_Site "World Heritage Site") in 2010

The [COVID-19 pandemic in Tasmania](/wiki/COVID-19_pandemic_in_Tasmania "COVID-19 pandemic in Tasmania") resulted in at least 230 cases and 13 deaths as of September 2021[[update]](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Tasmania&action=edit).[[73]](#cite_note-73) In 2020, after the outbreak of the [coronavirus pandemic](/wiki/Coronavirus_disease_2019 "Coronavirus disease 2019") ([SARS-CoV-2](/wiki/Severe_acute_respiratory_syndrome_coronavirus_2 "Severe acute respiratory syndrome coronavirus 2")) and its spread to Australia, the [Tasmanian government](/wiki/Tasmanian_Government "Tasmanian Government") issued a public health emergency on 17 March,[[74]](#cite_note-74) the following month receiving the state's most significant outbreak from the [North-West](/wiki/North_West_Tasmania "North West Tasmania") which required assistance from the [Federal government](/wiki/Australian_Government "Australian Government"). In late 2021, Tasmania was leading the nationwide vaccination response.[[75]](#cite_note-75)

## Geography

Main article: [Geography of Tasmania](/wiki/Geography_of_Tasmania "Geography of Tasmania")

|  |  |
| --- | --- |
|  | This article or section **may need to be cleaned up or summarized.** It has been split from/to *[Geography of Tasmania](/wiki/Geography_of_Tasmania "Geography of Tasmania")*. |

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/21/Topography_of_Tasmania.jpg/250px-Topography_of_Tasmania.jpg)](/wiki/File:Topography_of_Tasmania.jpg)

Topography of Tasmania

Tasmania, the largest island of Australia, has a landmass of 68,401 km2 (26,410 sq mi) and is located directly in the pathway of the notorious "[Roaring Forties](/wiki/Roaring_Forties "Roaring Forties")" wind that encircles the globe. To its north, it is separated from mainland Australia by [Bass Strait](/wiki/Bass_Strait "Bass Strait"). Tasmania is the only Australian state that is not located on the Australian mainland. About 2,500 kilometres (1,300 nautical miles) south of Tasmania island lies the [George V Coast](/wiki/George_V_Coast "George V Coast") of [Antarctica](/wiki/Antarctica "Antarctica"). Depending on which [borders of the oceans](/wiki/Borders_of_the_oceans "Borders of the oceans") are used, the island can be said to be either surrounded by the Southern Ocean, or to have the Pacific on its east and the Indian to its west. Still other definitions of the ocean boundaries would have Tasmania with the [Great Australian Bight](/wiki/Great_Australian_Bight "Great Australian Bight") to the west, and the [Tasman Sea](/wiki/Tasman_Sea "Tasman Sea") to the east. The southernmost point on mainland Tasmania is approximately [43°38′37″S 146°49′38″E﻿ / ﻿43.64361°S 146.82722°E﻿ / -43.64361; 146.82722](https://geohack.toolforge.org/geohack.php?pagename=Tasmania&params=43_38_37_S_146_49_38_E_) at [South East Cape](/wiki/South_East_Cape "South East Cape"), and the northernmost point on mainland Tasmania is approximately [40°38′26″S 144°43′33″E﻿ / ﻿40.64056°S 144.72583°E﻿ / -40.64056; 144.72583](https://geohack.toolforge.org/geohack.php?pagename=Tasmania&params=40_38_26_S_144_43_33_E_) in [Woolnorth / Temdudheker](/wiki/Woolnorth_/_Temdudheker,_Tasmania "Woolnorth / Temdudheker, Tasmania") near [Cape Grim / Kennaook](/wiki/Cape_Grim/Kennaook "Cape Grim/Kennaook"). Tasmania lies at similar latitudes to [Te Waipounamu / South Island](/wiki/South_Island "South Island") of New Zealand and parts of [Patagonia](/wiki/Patagonia "Patagonia") in South America. Areas at equivalent latitudes in the Northern Hemisphere include [Hokkaido](/wiki/Hokkaido "Hokkaido") in Japan, [Northeast China](/wiki/Northeast_China "Northeast China") ([Manchuria](/wiki/Manchuria "Manchuria")), [Central Italy](/wiki/Central_Italy "Central Italy"), and United States cities such as [New York](/wiki/New_York_City "New York City") and [Chicago](/wiki/Chicago "Chicago").

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/1a/Mortimer_Bay-3.jpg/330px-Mortimer_Bay-3.jpg)](/wiki/File:Mortimer_Bay-3.jpg)

Due to Tasmania's proximity to the [south magnetic pole](/wiki/South_magnetic_pole "South magnetic pole") towards [Antarctica](/wiki/Antarctica "Antarctica"), the [*Aurora australis*](/wiki/Aurora "Aurora") can sometimes be seen.

The most mountainous region is the [Central Highlands](/wiki/Central_Highlands_(Tasmania) "Central Highlands (Tasmania)") area, which covers most of the central western parts of the state. The [Midlands](/wiki/Midlands_(Tasmania) "Midlands (Tasmania)") located in the central east, is fairly flat, and is predominantly used for agriculture, although farming activity is scattered throughout the state. Tasmania's tallest mountain is [Mount Ossa](/wiki/Mount_Ossa_(Tasmania) "Mount Ossa (Tasmania)") at 1,617 m (5,305 ft).[[76]](#cite_note-76) Much of Tasmania is still densely forested, with the [Southwest National Park](/wiki/Southwest_National_Park "Southwest National Park") and neighbouring areas holding some of the last temperate rain forests in the [Southern Hemisphere](/wiki/Southern_Hemisphere "Southern Hemisphere"). The [Tarkine](/wiki/Tarkine "Tarkine"), containing [Savage River National Park](/wiki/Savage_River_National_Park "Savage River National Park") located in the island's far north west, is the largest [temperate rainforest](/wiki/Temperate_rainforest "Temperate rainforest") area in Australia covering about 3,800 square kilometres (1,500 sq mi).[[77]](#cite_note-77) With its rugged topography, Tasmania has a great number of rivers. Several of Tasmania's largest rivers have been dammed at some point to provide [hydroelectricity](/wiki/Hydroelectricity "Hydroelectricity"). Many rivers begin in the Central Highlands and flow out to the coast. Tasmania's major population centres are mainly situated around [estuaries](/wiki/Estuaries "Estuaries") (some of which are named rivers).

Tasmania is in the shape of a downward-facing triangle, likened to a shield, heart, or face. It consists of the main island as well as at least a thousand neighbouring islands within the state's jurisdiction. The largest of these are [Flinders Island](/wiki/Flinders_Island "Flinders Island") in the [Furneaux Group](/wiki/Furneaux_Group "Furneaux Group") of [Bass Strait](/wiki/Bass_Strait "Bass Strait"), [King Island](/wiki/King_Island_(Tasmania) "King Island (Tasmania)") in the west of Bass Strait, [Cape Barren Island](/wiki/Cape_Barren_Island "Cape Barren Island") south of Flinders Island, [Bruny Island](/wiki/Bruny_Island "Bruny Island") separated from Tasmania by the [D'Entrecasteaux Channel](/wiki/D%27Entrecasteaux_Channel "D'Entrecasteaux Channel"), [Macquarie Island](/wiki/Macquarie_Island "Macquarie Island") 1,500 km from Tasmania, and [Maria Island](/wiki/Maria_Island "Maria Island") off the east coast.
Tasmania features a number of separated and continuous mountain ranges. The majority of the state is defined by a significant [dolerite](/wiki/Diabase "Diabase") exposure, though the [western half](/wiki/Western_Tasmania "Western Tasmania") of the state is older and more rugged, featuring [buttongrass](/wiki/Buttongrass "Buttongrass") plains, temperate rainforests, and [quartzite](/wiki/Quartzite "Quartzite") ranges, notably [Federation Peak](/wiki/Federation_Peak "Federation Peak") and [Frenchmans Cap](/wiki/Frenchmans_Cap "Frenchmans Cap"). The presence of these mountain ranges is a primary factor in the [rain shadow effect](/wiki/Rain_shadow "Rain shadow"), where the western half receives the majority of rainfall, which also influences the types of vegetation that can grow. The Central Highlands feature a large plateau which forms a number of ranges and escarpments on its north side, tapering off along the south, and radiating into the highest mountain ranges in the west. At the north-west of this, another plateau radiates into a system of hills where [takayna / Tarkine](/wiki/Tarkine "Tarkine") is located.

The  [Interim Biogeographic Regionalisation for Australia (IBRA)](/wiki/Interim_Biogeographic_Regionalisation_for_Australia "Interim Biogeographic Regionalisation for Australia") divides Tasmania into 9 bioregions:[[78]](#cite_note-78)
[Ben Lomond](/wiki/Ben_Lomond_bioregion "Ben Lomond bioregion"), [Furneaux](/wiki/Furneaux_bioregion "Furneaux bioregion"), [King](/wiki/King_bioregion "King bioregion"), [Central Highlands](/wiki/Tasmanian_Central_Highlands "Tasmanian Central Highlands"), [Northern Midlands](/wiki/Tasmanian_Northern_Midlands "Tasmanian Northern Midlands"), [Northern Slopes](/wiki/Tasmanian_Northern_Slopes "Tasmanian Northern Slopes"), [Southern Ranges](/wiki/Tasmanian_Southern_Ranges "Tasmanian Southern Ranges"), [South East](/wiki/Tasmanian_South_East "Tasmanian South East"), and [West](/wiki/Tasmanian_West "Tasmanian West").

[Wineglass Bay](/wiki/Wineglass_Bay "Wineglass Bay") seen from Mount Amos at [Freycinet National Park](/wiki/Freycinet_National_Park "Freycinet National Park")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/bb/TAS_04-08-20.jpg/250px-TAS_04-08-20.jpg)](/wiki/File:TAS_04-08-20.jpg)

Satellite image showing snow covering Tasmania's highlands, August 2020

Tasmania's environment consistes of many different biomes or communities across its different regions. It is the most forested state in Australia, and preserves the country's largest areas of [temperate rainforest](/wiki/Temperate_rainforest "Temperate rainforest"). A distinctive type of [moorland](/wiki/Moorland "Moorland") found across the west, and particularly south-west of Tasmania, are [buttongrass](/wiki/Buttongrass "Buttongrass") plains, which are speculated to have been expanded by [Tasmanian Aboriginal](/wiki/Tasmanian_Aboriginal "Tasmanian Aboriginal") [burning practices](/wiki/Fire-stick_farming "Fire-stick farming").[[79]](#cite_note-79) Tasmania also features a diverse [alpine garden](/wiki/Alpine_plant "Alpine plant") [environment](/wiki/Alpine_vegetation_of_Tasmania "Alpine vegetation of Tasmania"), such as [cushion plant](/wiki/Tasmanian_cushion_plants "Tasmanian cushion plants"). Highland areas receive consistent [snowfall](/wiki/Snow "Snow") above ~1,000 metres every year, and due to cold air from [Antarctica](/wiki/Antarctica "Antarctica"), this level often reaches 800 m, and more occasionally 600 or 400 metres. Every five or so years, snow can form at sea level.[[80]](#cite_note-80) This environment gives rise to the [cypress](/wiki/Cypress "Cypress") forests of the [Central Plateau](/wiki/Central_Highlands_(Tasmania) "Central Highlands (Tasmania)") and mountainous highlands. In particular, the [Walls of Jerusalem](/wiki/Walls_of_Jerusalem_National_Park "Walls of Jerusalem National Park") with large areas of rare [pencil pine](/wiki/Athrotaxis_cupressoides "Athrotaxis cupressoides"), and its closest relative [King Billy pine](/wiki/Athrotaxis_selaginoides "Athrotaxis selaginoides"). On the [West Coast Range](/wiki/West_Coast_Range "West Coast Range") and partially on [Mount Field](/wiki/Mount_Field_National_Park "Mount Field National Park"), Australia's only winter-[deciduous](/wiki/Deciduous "Deciduous") plant, [deciduous beech](/wiki/Nothofagus_gunnii "Nothofagus gunnii") is found, which forms a carpet or [krummholz](/wiki/Krummholz "Krummholz"), or very rarely a 4-metre tree.[[81]](#cite_note-81)

Tasmania features a high concentration of [waterfalls](/wiki/Waterfalls "Waterfalls"). These can be found in small creeks, alpine [streams](/wiki/Streams "Streams"), [rapid](/wiki/Rapids "Rapids") [rivers](/wiki/Rivers "Rivers"), or off precipitous plunges. Some of the tallest waterfalls are found on mountain [massifs](/wiki/Massifs "Massifs"), sometimes at a 200-metre cascade. The most famous and most visited waterfall in Tasmania is [Russell Falls](/wiki/Russell_Falls "Russell Falls") in [Mount Field](/wiki/Mount_Field_National_Park "Mount Field National Park") due to its proximity to [Hobart](/wiki/Hobart "Hobart") and stepped falls at a total height of 58 metres.[[82]](#cite_note-82)
Tasmania also has a large number of [beaches](/wiki/Beaches "Beaches"), the longest of which is [Ocean Beach](/wiki/Ocean_Beach_(Tasmania) "Ocean Beach (Tasmania)") on the [West Coast](/wiki/West_Coast,_Tasmania "West Coast, Tasmania") at about 40 kilometres.[[83]](#cite_note-83) Wineglass Bay in [Freycinet](/wiki/Freycinet_National_Park "Freycinet National Park") on the east coast is a well-known [landmark](/wiki/Landmark "Landmark") of the state.

The [Tasmanian temperate rainforests](/wiki/Tasmanian_temperate_rainforests "Tasmanian temperate rainforests") cover a few different types. These are also considered distinct from the more common wet [sclerophyll](/wiki/Sclerophyll "Sclerophyll") forests, though these [eucalypt](/wiki/Eucalyptus "Eucalyptus") forests often form with [rainforest](/wiki/Rainforest "Rainforest") [understorey](/wiki/Understorey "Understorey") and [ferns](/wiki/Ferns "Ferns") (such as [tree-ferns](/wiki/Man_fern "Man fern")) are not usually absent. Rainforest found in deep [gullies](/wiki/Gully "Gully") are usually difficult to traverse due to dense understorey growth, such as from [horizontal (*Anodopetalum biglandulosum*)](/wiki/Anodopetalum_biglandulosum "Anodopetalum biglandulosum"). Higher-elevation forests (~500 to 800 m) have smaller ground vegetation and are thus easier to walk in. The most common rainforests usually have a 50-metre[[84]](#cite_note-84) [canopy](/wiki/Canopy_(biology) "Canopy (biology)") and are varied by environmental factors. Emergent growth usually comes from [eucalyptus](/wiki/Eucalyptus_regnans "Eucalyptus regnans"), which can tower another 50 metres higher (usually less), providing the most common choice of nesting for giant [wedge-tailed eagles](/wiki/Tasmanian_wedge-tailed_eagle "Tasmanian wedge-tailed eagle").

The human environment ranges from urban or industrial development to farming or grazing land. The most cultivated area is the [Midlands](/wiki/Midlands_(Tasmania) "Midlands (Tasmania)"), where it has suitable soil but is also the driest part of the state.

Tasmania's [insularity](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/insular#Adjective "wiktionary:insular") was possibly detected by Captain [Abel Tasman](/wiki/Abel_Tasman "Abel Tasman") when he charted Tasmania's coast in 1642. On 5 December, Tasman was following the [east coast](/wiki/East_Coast_of_Australia "East Coast of Australia") northward to see how far it went. When the land veered to the north-west at [Eddystone Point](/wiki/Eddystone_Point "Eddystone Point"),[[85]](#cite_note-85) he tried to keep in with it but his ships were suddenly hit by the [Roaring Forties](/wiki/Roaring_Forties "Roaring Forties") howling through [Bass Strait](/wiki/Bass_Strait "Bass Strait").[[86]](#cite_note-86) Tasman was on a mission to find the [Southern Continent](/wiki/Terra_Australis "Terra Australis"), not more islands, so he abruptly turned away to the east and continued his continent-hunting.[[87]](#cite_note-87)

The next European to enter the strait was Captain [James Cook](/wiki/James_Cook "James Cook") on [HMS *Endeavour*](/wiki/HMS_Endeavour "HMS Endeavour") in April 1770. However, after sailing for two hours westward into the strait against the wind, he turned back east and noted in his journal that he was "doubtful whether they [i.e. Van Diemen's Land and New Holland] are one land or no".[[88]](#cite_note-88)

The strait was named after George Bass, after he and Matthew Flinders passed through it while circumnavigating Van Diemen's Land in the *Norfolk* in 1798–99. At Flinders' recommendation, the Governor of New South Wales, John Hunter, in 1800 named the stretch of water between the mainland and Van Diemen's Land "Bass's Straits".[[89]](#cite_note-89) Later it became known as Bass Strait.

The existence of the strait had been suggested in 1797 by the master of Sydney Cove when he reached Sydney after deliberately grounding his foundering ship and being stranded on Preservation Island (at the eastern end of the strait). He reported that the strong south westerly swell and the tides and currents suggested that the island was in a channel linking the Pacific and southern Indian Ocean. Governor Hunter thus wrote to Joseph Banks in August 1797 that it seemed certain a strait existed.[[90]](#cite_note-90)

### Climate

See also: [Climate of Tasmania](/wiki/Climate_of_Tasmania "Climate of Tasmania")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/43/CradleMountainTas_b.jpg/250px-CradleMountainTas_b.jpg)](/wiki/File:CradleMountainTas_b.jpg)

Snow on [Cradle Mountain](/wiki/Cradle_Mountain "Cradle Mountain")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/85/Tasmania_K%C3%B6ppen.svg/250px-Tasmania_K%C3%B6ppen.svg.png)](/wiki/File:Tasmania_K%C3%B6ppen.svg)

The Köppen climate classifications of Tasmania

Tasmania has a relatively cool temperate climate compared to the rest of Australia, spared from the hot summers of the mainland and experiencing four distinct seasons.[[91]](#cite_note-91) Summer is from December to February when the average maximum sea temperature is 21 °C (70 °F) and inland areas around Launceston reach 24 °C (75 °F). Other inland areas are much cooler, with [Liawenee](/wiki/Liawenee "Liawenee"), located on the Central Plateau, one of the coldest places in Australia, ranging between 4 and 17 °C (39 and 63 °F) in February. Autumn is from March to May, with mostly settled weather, as summer patterns gradually take on the shape of winter patterns.[[92]](#cite_note-92) The winter months are from June to August and are generally the wettest and coldest months in the state, with most high lying areas receiving considerable snowfall. Winter maximums are 12 °C (54 °F) on average along coastal areas and 3 °C (37 °F) on the central plateau, as a result of a series of cold fronts from the [Southern Ocean](/wiki/Southern_Ocean "Southern Ocean"). Inland areas receive regular freezes throughout the winter months. Spring is from September to November, and is an unsettled season of transition, where winter weather patterns begin to take the shape of summer patterns, although snowfall is still common up until October. Spring is generally the windiest time of the year with afternoon sea breezes starting to take effect on the coast.

### Biodiversity

Main article: [Ecology of Tasmania](/wiki/Ecology_of_Tasmania "Ecology of Tasmania")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/cb/Hellyer_Gorge%2C_Tasmania.jpg/250px-Hellyer_Gorge%2C_Tasmania.jpg)](/wiki/File:Hellyer_Gorge,_Tasmania.jpg)

[Ferns](/wiki/Fern "Fern") in [Hellyer Gorge](/wiki/Hellyer_Gorge "Hellyer Gorge"), to the northeast of [Savage River National Park](/wiki/Savage_River_National_Park "Savage River National Park")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/43/Sarcophilus_harrisii_taranna.jpg/250px-Sarcophilus_harrisii_taranna.jpg)](/wiki/File:Sarcophilus_harrisii_taranna.jpg)

The [Tasmanian Devil](/wiki/Tasmanian_Devil "Tasmanian Devil"), Tasmania's state animal emblem

Geographically and biological isolated, Tasmania is known for its unique [endemic](/wiki/Endemic "Endemic") flora and fauna.

#### Flora

Tasmania has [extremely diverse](/wiki/Biodiversity "Biodiversity") vegetation, from the heavily grazed grassland of the dry Midlands to the tall evergreen [eucalypt](/wiki/Eucalypt "Eucalypt") forest, [alpine](/wiki/Alpine_climate "Alpine climate") heathlands and large areas of cool [temperate rainforests](/wiki/Temperate_rain_forest "Temperate rain forest") and moorlands in the rest of the state. Many species are unique to Tasmania, and some are related to species in South America and New Zealand through ancestors which grew on the supercontinent of [Gondwana](/wiki/Gondwana "Gondwana"), 50 million years ago. *[Nothofagus gunnii](/wiki/Nothofagus_gunnii "Nothofagus gunnii")*, commonly known as Australian beech, is Australia's only temperate native deciduous tree and is found exclusively in Tasmania.[[105]](#cite_note-105)

Distinctive species of plant in Tasmania include:

* [*Eucalyptus regnans* (mountain ash)](/wiki/Eucalyptus_regnans "Eucalyptus regnans") – the [tallest](/wiki/List_of_superlative_trees "List of superlative trees") flowering plant and hardwood in the world, [reaching 100 m](/wiki/Centurion_(tree) "Centurion (tree)") (328 ft).[[106]](#cite_note-106)
* [*Nothofagus cunninghamii* (myrtle beech)](/wiki/Nothofagus_cunninghamii "Nothofagus cunninghamii") – the most abundant temperate rainforest canopy species found in Tasmania.
* [*Nothofagus gunnii* (deciduous beech)](/wiki/Nothofagus_gunnii "Nothofagus gunnii") – Australia's only winter-deciduous tree.
* [*Atherosperma moschatum* (blackheart sassafras)](/wiki/Atherosperma_moschatum "Atherosperma moschatum") – a co-dominant rainforest tree with a nutmeg aroma.
* [*Lagarostrobos franklinii* (Huon pine)](/wiki/Lagarostrobos_franklinii "Lagarostrobos franklinii") – one of the oldest-lived tree species, and a self-preserving timber.
* [*Phyllocladus aspleniifolius* (celery-top pine)](/wiki/Phyllocladus_aspleniifolius "Phyllocladus aspleniifolius") – a celery-leaved conifer found in rainforests.
* [Athrotaxis (Tasmanian cedar/redwood)](/wiki/Athrotaxis "Athrotaxis") – a genus comprising three extant species related to [sequoia](/wiki/Sequoiadendron "Sequoiadendron") found in Tasmania.[[107]](#cite_note-107)
* [*Eucryphia lucida* (leatherwood)](/wiki/Eucryphia_lucida "Eucryphia lucida") – a prominent floral symbol of Tasmania and a unique [monofloral honey](/wiki/Monofloral_honey "Monofloral honey") species.[[108]](#cite_note-108)

##### Bush tucker

See also: [Bush tucker § Tasmania](/wiki/Bush_tucker#Tasmania "Bush tucker")

Tasmania also has a number of [native](/wiki/Native_species "Native species") [edibles](/wiki/Edible "Edible"), known as [bush tucker](/wiki/Bush_tucker "Bush tucker") in Australia. These plants were [foraged](/wiki/Foraging "Foraging") by the [Tasmanian Aboriginals](/wiki/Tasmanian_Aboriginals "Tasmanian Aboriginals") and also used for other purposes, such as [construction](/wiki/Construction "Construction"). Unusual trees such as [cider gum (*Eucalyptus gunnii*)](/wiki/Eucalyptus_gunnii "Eucalyptus gunnii") had their [manna](/wiki/Sap "Sap") used to make a [syrup](/wiki/Syrup "Syrup") or an [alcohol](/wiki/Alcohol_(drug) "Alcohol (drug)") ([cider](/wiki/Cider "Cider")). Other trees such as [wattles (acacias)](/wiki/Acacia "Acacia") like [blackwood (*Acacia melanoxylon*)](/wiki/Acacia_melanoxylon "Acacia melanoxylon") and [mimosa (*Acacia dealbata*)](/wiki/Acacia_dealbata "Acacia dealbata") could have their [seeds](/wiki/Wattleseed "Wattleseed") eaten or crushed into a [powder](/wiki/Powder "Powder"). There are also many [berries](/wiki/Berries "Berries") such as [snowberry (*Gaultheria hispida*)](/wiki/Gaultheria_hispida "Gaultheria hispida"), [fruits](/wiki/Fruits "Fruits") such as [heartberry (*Aristotelia peduncularis*)](/wiki/Aristotelia_peduncularis "Aristotelia peduncularis"), and [vegetables](/wiki/Vegetables "Vegetables") such as [river mint (*Mentha australis*)](/wiki/Mentha_australis "Mentha australis"), though no [crops](/wiki/Crops "Crops") like [maize](/wiki/Maize "Maize") that are used for large production.[[109]](#cite_note-109)

#### Fauna

Tasmania has a large percentage of [endemism](/wiki/Endemism "Endemism") whilst featuring many types of animals found on mainland Australia. Many of these species, such as the [platypus](/wiki/Platypus "Platypus"), are larger than their mainland relatives.[[110]](#cite_note-110)
The island of Tasmania was home to the [thylacine](/wiki/Thylacine "Thylacine"), a [marsupial](/wiki/Marsupial "Marsupial") which resembled a [fossa](/wiki/Fossa_(animal) "Fossa (animal)") or some say a wild dog. Known colloquially as the Tasmanian tiger for the distinctive striping across its back, it became extinct in mainland Australia much earlier because of competition by the [dingo](/wiki/Dingo "Dingo"), introduced in prehistoric times. Owing to persecution by farmers, government-funded bounty hunters and, in the final years, collectors for overseas museums, it appears to have been exterminated in Tasmania. The [Tasmanian devil](/wiki/Tasmanian_devil "Tasmanian devil") became the [largest carnivorous marsupial](/wiki/Largest_mammals#Marsupials_(Marsupialia) "Largest mammals") in the world following the [extinction](/wiki/Extinction "Extinction") of the [thylacine](/wiki/Thylacine "Thylacine") in 1936 and is now found in the wild only in Tasmania. Tasmania was one of the last regions of Australia to be introduced to domesticated dogs. Dogs were brought from Britain in 1803 for hunting kangaroos and [emus](/wiki/Emu "Emu"). This introduction completely transformed Aboriginal society, as it helped them to successfully compete with European hunters and was more important than the introduction of guns for the Aboriginal people.[[111]](#cite_note-111)

Tasmania is a hotspot for [giant habitat trees and the large animal species](/wiki/Island_gigantism "Island gigantism") that occupy them, notably the endangered [Tasmanian wedge-tailed eagle (*Aquila audax fleayi*)](/wiki/Tasmanian_wedge-tailed_eagle "Tasmanian wedge-tailed eagle"), the [Tasmanian masked owl (*Tyto novaehollandiae castanops*)](/wiki/Tasmanian_masked_owl "Tasmanian masked owl"), the [Tasmanian giant freshwater crayfish (*Astacopsis gouldi*)](/wiki/Tasmanian_giant_freshwater_crayfish "Tasmanian giant freshwater crayfish"), the [yellow wattlebird (*Anthochaera paradoxa*)](/wiki/Yellow_wattlebird "Yellow wattlebird"), the [green rosella (*Platycercus caledonicus*)](/wiki/Green_rosella "Green rosella") and others. Tasmania is also home to the world's only three migratory parrots, the critically endangered [Orange-bellied parrot (*Neophema chrysogaster*)](/wiki/Orange-bellied_parrot "Orange-bellied parrot"), the [Blue-winged parrot (*Neophema chrysostoma*)](/wiki/Blue-winged_parrot "Blue-winged parrot"), and the fastest parrot in the world, the [swift parrot (*Lathamus discolor*)](/wiki/Swift_parrot "Swift parrot").[[112]](#cite_note-112)
Tasmania has 12 [endemic](/wiki/Endemic "Endemic") species of [bird](/wiki/Bird "Bird") in total.[[113]](#cite_note-113)

#### Mycology

Tasmania is a hotspot for [fungal](/wiki/Fungi "Fungi") diversity. The importance of fungi in Tasmania's ecology is often overlooked; nonetheless, they play a vital role in the natural vegetation cycle.[[114]](#cite_note-114) Tasmania's southwestern wilderness hosts a rich diversity of alpine [lichens](/wiki/Lichen "Lichen"), with at least 162 documented species. The region's alpine lichen flora is particularly notable for its high level of endemism, with approximately 16% of species found nowhere else in the world. The area's cool [maritime climate](/wiki/Maritime_climate "Maritime climate"), Precambrian geology, and extensive [peat](/wiki/Peat "Peat") formations create unique habitats for these organisms. The lichen [communities](/wiki/Community_(ecology) "Community (ecology)") differ significantly from those found on Tasmania's eastern dolerite mountains, with the southwestern species showing stronger affinities to New Zealand and sub-Antarctic flora rather than to mainland Australia. Major lichen habitats in the alpine southwest include heathlands, alpine lawns, [feldmark](/wiki/Feldmark "Feldmark") (windswept rocky plateaus), and large rock outcrops, each supporting distinct assemblages of species. Some particularly diverse genera in the region include *[Bunodophoron](/wiki/Bunodophoron "Bunodophoron")*, *[Cladia](/wiki/Cladia "Cladia")*, *[Cladonia](/wiki/Cladonia "Cladonia")*, *[Menegazzia](/wiki/Menegazzia "Menegazzia")*, *[Micarea](/wiki/Micarea "Micarea")*, *[Pertusaria](/wiki/Pertusaria "Pertusaria")*, *[Pseudocyphellaria](/wiki/Pseudocyphellaria "Pseudocyphellaria")*, *[Psoroma](/wiki/Psoroma "Psoroma")*, *[Siphula](/wiki/Siphula "Siphula")*, and *[Stereocaulon](/wiki/Stereocaulon "Stereocaulon")*.[[115]](#cite_note-Kantvilas_1995-115)

#### Conservation

Like the rest of Australia, Tasmania suffers from an [endangered species](/wiki/Endangered_species "Endangered species") problem. In particular, many important Tasmanian subspecies and world-significant species of [animal](/wiki/Animal "Animal") are classified as at risk in some way. A famous example is the [Tasmanian devil](/wiki/Tasmanian_devil "Tasmanian devil"), which is endangered due to [devil facial tumour disease](/wiki/Devil_facial_tumour_disease "Devil facial tumour disease"). Some species have already gone [extinct](/wiki/Extinct "Extinct"), primarily due to [human interference](/wiki/Human_impact_on_the_environment "Human impact on the environment"), such as in the case of the [thylacine](/wiki/Thylacine "Thylacine") or the [Tasmanian emu](/wiki/Tasmanian_emu "Tasmanian emu").[[116]](#cite_note-116)[[117]](#cite_note-117) In Tasmania, there are about 90 endangered, vulnerable, or threatened vertebrate species classified by the state or Commonwealth governments.[[118]](#cite_note-118) Because of a reliance on roads and private vehicle transport, and a high concentration of animal populations divided by this development, Tasmania has the worst (per kilometre) [roadkill](/wiki/Roadkill "Roadkill") rate in the world, with 32 animals killed per hour and at least 300,000 per year.[[119]](#cite_note-119)

[Protected areas of Tasmania](/wiki/Protected_areas_of_Tasmania "Protected areas of Tasmania") cover 21% of the island's land area in the form of [national parks](/wiki/National_park "National park").[[120]](#cite_note-120) The [Tasmanian Wilderness World Heritage Area](/wiki/Tasmanian_Wilderness_World_Heritage_Area "Tasmanian Wilderness World Heritage Area") (TWWHA) was inscribed by [UNESCO](/wiki/UNESCO "UNESCO") in 1982, where it is globally significant because "most UNESCO World Heritage sites meet only one or two of the ten criteria for that status. The Tasmanian Wilderness World Heritage Area (TWWHA) meets 7 out of 10 criteria. Only one other place on earth—China’s [Mount Taishan](/wiki/Mount_Tai "Mount Tai")—meets that many criteria".[[121]](#cite_note-121)
Controversy surrounds the decision in 2014 by the [Abbott](/wiki/Abbott_Government "Abbott Government") federal [Liberal government](/wiki/Liberal_Party_of_Australia "Liberal Party of Australia") to request the area's delisting and opening for resource exploration (before it was rejected by the UN Committee at Doha),[[122]](#cite_note-122) and the current [mining](/wiki/Mining "Mining") and [deforestation](/wiki/Deforestation "Deforestation") in the state's [Tarkine](/wiki/Tarkine "Tarkine") region, the largest single [temperate rainforest](/wiki/Temperate_rainforest "Temperate rainforest") in Australia.[[123]](#cite_note-123)[[124]](#cite_note-124)

## Demography

"Tasmanian people" redirects here. For the original inhabitants of Tasmania, see [§ Aboriginal people](#Aboriginal_people), and [Aboriginal Tasmanians](/wiki/Aboriginal_Tasmanians "Aboriginal Tasmanians").

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/22/ABS-3101.0-AustralianDemographicStatistics-EstimatedResidentPopulationStatesTerritories-EstimatedResidentPopulation-Persons-Tasmania-A2060848V.svg/250px-ABS-3101.0-AustralianDemographicStatistics-EstimatedResidentPopulationStatesTerritories-EstimatedResidentPopulation-Persons-Tasmania-A2060848V.svg.png)](/wiki/File:ABS-3101.0-AustralianDemographicStatistics-EstimatedResidentPopulationStatesTerritories-EstimatedResidentPopulation-Persons-Tasmania-A2060848V.svg)

Estimated resident population since 1981

The population of Tasmania is the most homogeneous of any Australian state, being mostly of [British](/wiki/United_Kingdom_of_Great_Britain_and_Ireland "United Kingdom of Great Britain and Ireland") (primarily [English](/wiki/English_people "English people")) descent.[[125]](#cite_note-125)

Until 2012, Tasmania was the only state in Australia with an above-replacement [total fertility rate](/wiki/Total_fertility_rate "Total fertility rate"); Tasmanian women had an average of 2.24 children each.[[126]](#cite_note-126) By 2012 the birth rate had slipped to 2.1 children per woman, bringing the state to the replacement threshold, but it continues to have the second-highest birth rate of any state or territory (behind the Northern Territory).[[127]](#cite_note-127)

Major population centres include [Hobart](/wiki/Hobart "Hobart"), [Launceston](/wiki/Launceston,_Tasmania "Launceston, Tasmania"), [Devonport](/wiki/Devonport,_Tasmania "Devonport, Tasmania"), [Burnie](/wiki/Burnie,_Tasmania "Burnie, Tasmania"), and [Ulverstone](/wiki/Ulverstone,_Tasmania "Ulverstone, Tasmania"). [Kingston](/wiki/Kingston,_Tasmania "Kingston, Tasmania") is often defined as a separate city but is generally regarded as part of the Greater Hobart Area.[[128]](#cite_note-128)

The five largest groups of overseas-born were from England (3.7%), New Zealand (1%), [Mainland China](/wiki/Mainland_China "Mainland China") (0.6%), Scotland (0.4%) and the Netherlands (0.4%).[[132]](#cite_note-quickstats.censusdata.abs.gov.au-132)[[133]](#cite_note-censusdata.abs.gov.au-133)

4.6% of the population, or 23,572 people, identified as [Indigenous Australians](/wiki/Indigenous_Australians "Indigenous Australians") ([Aboriginal Australians](/wiki/Aboriginal_Australians "Aboriginal Australians") and [Torres Strait Islanders](/wiki/Torres_Strait_Islanders "Torres Strait Islanders")) in 2016.[[N 5]](#cite_note-139)[[132]](#cite_note-quickstats.censusdata.abs.gov.au-132)[[133]](#cite_note-censusdata.abs.gov.au-133)

### Language

At the [2021 census](/wiki/2021_Australian_census "2021 Australian census"), 86.1% of inhabitants spoke only English at home, with the next most common languages being [Mandarin](/wiki/Mandarin_Chinese "Mandarin Chinese") (1.5%), [Nepali](/wiki/Nepali_language "Nepali language") (1.3%), [Punjabi](/wiki/Punjabi_language "Punjabi language") (0.5%) and Spanish (0.3%).[[135]](#cite_note-census2021-140)

In 2021, 50.0% of people in Tasmania reported having no religious affiliation, a substantial increase from 38.2% in 2016 and just 5.1% in 1971. Meanwhile, Christianity remained the largest religious affiliation in the state, with 38.4% identifying as Christian, though this proportion has steadily declined over time—from 88.7% in 1971 to 49.7% in 2016.[[135]](#cite_note-census2021-140)

Non-Christian religions accounted for 4.5% of the population in 2021, with Hinduism (1.7%), Buddhism (1.0%), and Islam (0.9%) being the most prevalent among them.[[135]](#cite_note-census2021-140)[[132]](#cite_note-quickstats.censusdata.abs.gov.au-132)[[133]](#cite_note-censusdata.abs.gov.au-133)

## Government

Main article: [Government of Tasmania](/wiki/Government_of_Tasmania "Government of Tasmania")

Further information: [Governors of Tasmania](/wiki/List_of_Governors_of_Tasmania "List of Governors of Tasmania") and [Parliament of Tasmania](/wiki/Parliament_of_Tasmania "Parliament of Tasmania")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/5b/Hobart-Tasmania-Australia04.JPG/250px-Hobart-Tasmania-Australia04.JPG)](/wiki/File:Hobart-Tasmania-Australia04.JPG)

[Parliament House, Hobart](/wiki/Parliament_House,_Hobart "Parliament House, Hobart")

The form of the government of Tasmania is prescribed in [its constitution](/wiki/Constitution_of_Tasmania "Constitution of Tasmania"), which dates from 1934. Since 1901, Tasmania has been a state of the Commonwealth of Australia, and the [Australian Constitution](/wiki/Australian_Constitution "Australian Constitution") regulates its relationship with the Commonwealth and prescribes which powers each level of government is allowed.

Tasmania is represented in the [Senate](/wiki/Australian_Senate "Australian Senate") by 12 senators, on an equal basis with all other states. In the [House of Representatives](/wiki/Australian_House_of_Representatives "Australian House of Representatives"), Tasmania is entitled to five seats, which is the minimum allocation for a state guaranteed by the Constitution—the number of House of Representatives seats for each state is otherwise decided on the basis of their relative populations, and Tasmania has never qualified for five seats on that basis alone. Tasmania's [House of Assembly](/wiki/Tasmanian_House_of_Assembly "Tasmanian House of Assembly") use a system of multi-seat [proportional representation](/wiki/Proportional_representation "Proportional representation") known as [Hare-Clark](/wiki/Single_Transferable_Vote "Single Transferable Vote").

### Parliamentary elections

At the [2002 state election](/wiki/2002_Tasmanian_state_election "2002 Tasmanian state election"), the [Labor Party](/wiki/Australian_Labor_Party "Australian Labor Party") won 14 of the 25 House seats. The people decreased their vote for the [Liberal Party](/wiki/Liberal_Party_of_Australia "Liberal Party of Australia"); representation in the Parliament fell to seven seats. The [Greens](/wiki/Tasmanian_Greens "Tasmanian Greens") won four seats, with over 18% of the popular vote, the highest proportion of any Green party in any parliament in the world at that time.

Tasmania in parliamentary elections has consecutively elected minority governments since [2024](/wiki/2024_Tasmanian_state_election "2024 Tasmanian state election"). The [premiership of Jeremy Rockliff](/wiki/Premiership_of_Jeremy_Rockliff "Premiership of Jeremy Rockliff") has retained government with the support of the crossbench.[[136]](#cite_note-141)

### Politics

Tasmania has a number of undeveloped regions. Proposals for local economic development have been faced with requirements for environmental sensitivity, or opposition. In particular, proposals for hydroelectric power generation were debated in the late 20th century. In the 1970s, opposition to the construction of the [Lake Pedder](/wiki/Lake_Pedder "Lake Pedder") reservoir impoundment led to the formation of the world's first [Green party](/wiki/Green_party "Green party"), the [United Tasmania Group](/wiki/United_Tasmania_Group "United Tasmania Group").[[137]](#cite_note-Lake_Pedder-142)

In the early 1980s the state debated the proposed [Franklin River Dam](/wiki/Franklin_Dam "Franklin Dam"). The anti-dam sentiment was shared by many Australians outside Tasmania and proved a factor in the election of the [Hawke](/wiki/Bob_Hawke "Bob Hawke") [Labor](/wiki/Australian_Labor_Party "Australian Labor Party") government in 1983, which halted construction of the dam.[[138]](#cite_note-143) Since the 1980s the environmental focus has shifted to [old growth](/wiki/Old_growth "Old growth") logging and mining in the Tarkine region, which have both proved divisive.[[139]](#cite_note-144) The Tasmania Together process recommended an end to clear felling in high conservation old growth forests by January 2003, but was unsuccessful.

In [1996](/wiki/1996_Tasmanian_state_election "1996 Tasmanian state election"), the House of Assembly consisted of 35 seats with 7 seats per each of the five electorates. By the [1998 election](/wiki/1998_Tasmanian_state_election "1998 Tasmanian state election"), the number of seats had been reduced down to 25, or 5 per each electorate. This resulted in the reduction of the Greens' number of seats from 4 to 1, and increased the proportion of seats held by both the Labor and Liberal parties.[[140]](#cite_note-145) This was despite growth in population (five-fold since responsible government) and an increase in the voting percentage required for a majority government. There was also no public consultation, and inquiries at the time had recommended the opposite. The House of Assembly Select Committee in 2020 recommended in its report that the number should be increased again from 25 to 35, arguing that such a small representation would undermine democracy and limit the capabilities of the government. In 2010, the major party leadership had even endorsed reinstating the 35 seat number, but Liberal and Labor support was withdrawn the following year, with only the Greens keeping their commitment.[[141]](#cite_note-146)
