### Local government

Main article: [Local government areas of Tasmania](/wiki/Local_government_areas_of_Tasmania "Local government areas of Tasmania")

Tasmania has 29 local government areas. Local councils are responsible for functions delegated by the Tasmanian parliament, such as urban planning, road infrastructure and waste management. Council revenue comes mostly from property taxes and government grants.

As with the [House of Assembly](/wiki/Tasmanian_House_of_Assembly "Tasmanian House of Assembly"), Tasmania's local government elections use a system of multi-seat [proportional representation](/wiki/Proportional_representation "Proportional representation") known as [Hare–Clark](/wiki/Single_transferable_vote "Single transferable vote"). Local government elections take place every four years and are conducted by the [Tasmanian Electoral Commission](/wiki/Tasmanian_Electoral_Commission "Tasmanian Electoral Commission") by full [postal ballot](/wiki/Postal_ballot "Postal ballot"). The next local government elections will be held during October 2026.[[142]](#cite_note-147)

## Economy

Main article: [Economy of Tasmania](/wiki/Economy_of_Tasmania "Economy of Tasmania")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/cb/Tasmanian_salmon_on_a_pea_and_corn_fritter.jpg/250px-Tasmanian_salmon_on_a_pea_and_corn_fritter.jpg)](/wiki/File:Tasmanian_salmon_on_a_pea_and_corn_fritter.jpg)

Smoked Tasmanian salmon. Tasmania is a large exporter of seafood, particularly [salmon](/wiki/Salmon "Salmon").

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/d6/Derwent_Valley_%2822645573102%29.jpg/250px-Derwent_Valley_%2822645573102%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Derwent_Valley_(22645573102).jpg)

[Hop farms](/wiki/Hops "Hops") in the [Derwent Valley](/wiki/Derwent_Valley_Council "Derwent Valley Council")

Traditionally, Tasmania's main industries have been mining (including copper, [zinc](/wiki/Zinc "Zinc"), [tin](/wiki/Tin "Tin"), and iron), agriculture, forestry, and [tourism](/wiki/Tourism_in_Tasmania "Tourism in Tasmania"). Tasmania is on [Australia's electrical grid](/wiki/Electricity_sector_in_Australia "Electricity sector in Australia") and in the 1940s and 1950s, a hydro-industrialisation initiative was embodied in the state by [Hydro Tasmania](/wiki/Hydro_Tasmania "Hydro Tasmania"). These all have had varying fortunes over the last century and more, involved in ebbs and flows of population moving in and away dependent upon the specific requirements of the dominant industries of the time.[[143]](#cite_note-:0-148) The state also has a large number of food exporting sectors, including but not limited to seafood (such as [salmon](/wiki/Salmon "Salmon"), [abalone](/wiki/Abalone "Abalone") and [crayfish](/wiki/Crayfish "Crayfish")).

In the 1960s and 1970s there was a decline in traditional crops such as apples and pears,[[144]](#cite_note-149) with other crops and industries eventually rising in their place. During the 15 years until 2010, new agricultural products such as wine, [saffron](/wiki/Saffron "Saffron"), [pyrethrum](/wiki/Pyrethrum "Pyrethrum") and [cherries](/wiki/Cherry "Cherry") have been fostered by the [Tasmanian Institute of Agricultural Research](/wiki/Tasmanian_Institute_of_Agricultural_Research "Tasmanian Institute of Agricultural Research").

Favourable economic conditions throughout Australia, cheaper air fares, and two new *Spirit of Tasmania* ferries have all contributed to what is now a rising tourism industry.

About 1.7% of the Tasmanian population are employed by local government.[[145]](#cite_note-150) Other major employers include [Nyrstar](/wiki/Nyrstar "Nyrstar"), [Norske Skog](/wiki/Norske_Skog "Norske Skog"), Grange Resources, [Rio Tinto](/wiki/Rio_Tinto_Group "Rio Tinto Group"),[[146]](#cite_note-151) the [Roman Catholic Archdiocese of Hobart](/wiki/Roman_Catholic_Archdiocese_of_Hobart "Roman Catholic Archdiocese of Hobart"), and [Federal Group](/wiki/Federal_Group "Federal Group"). Small business is a large part of the community life, including [Incat](/wiki/Incat "Incat"), [Moorilla Estate](/wiki/Moorilla_Estate "Moorilla Estate") and [Tassal](/wiki/Tassal "Tassal"). In the late 1990s, a number of national companies based their call centres in the state after obtaining cheap access to broad-band fibre optic connections.[[147]](#cite_note-152)[[143]](#cite_note-:0-148)

34% of Tasmanians are reliant on welfare payments as their primary source of income.[[148]](#cite_note-153) This number is in part due to the large number of older residents and retirees in Tasmania receiving Age Pensions. Due to its natural environment and clean air, Tasmania is a common retirement selection for Australians.[[149]](#cite_note-154)

### Science and technology

The modern [scientific](/wiki/Science "Science") sector in Tasmania benefits from around $500 million in annual investment.[[152]](#cite_note-157)
Tasmania has a long history of scientific and [technological](/wiki/Technology "Technology") [innovation](/wiki/Innovation "Innovation").[[153]](#cite_note-158) The first scientific-style [observations](/wiki/Observation "Observation") were conducted by the [First Nation Tasmanians](/wiki/Aboriginal_Tasmanians "Aboriginal Tasmanians"), primarily through the [watching](/wiki/Amateur_astronomy "Amateur astronomy") and [mythologising](/wiki/Myth "Myth") of the [night sky](/wiki/Night_sky "Night sky"). Their story explaining the [phases](/wiki/Lunar_phase "Lunar phase") of the [moon](/wiki/Moon "Moon") and [sun](/wiki/Sun "Sun") "is one of the rare accounts that explicitly acknowledges that the light of the Moon is a reflection of the Sun's light".[[154]](#cite_note-159)

The French [D'Entrecasteaux](/wiki/Antoine_Bruni_d%27Entrecasteaux "Antoine Bruni d'Entrecasteaux") Expedition of 1792–93 had anchored twice during its search of the missing [La Pérouse](/wiki/Jean-Fran%C3%A7ois_de_Galaup,_comte_de_Lap%C3%A9rouse "Jean-François de Galaup, comte de Lapérouse") in the [Baie de la Recherche (Recherche Bay)](/wiki/Recherche_Bay "Recherche Bay") in far-south Tasmania. During their stay, the crew took [botanical](/wiki/Botany "Botany"), [astronomical](/wiki/Astronomy "Astronomy"), and [geomagnetic](/wiki/Earth%27s_magnetic_field "Earth's magnetic field") observations which were the first of their kind performed on Australian soil. As well as this, they engaged in amicable relations with the locals and environment, gifting the area a "French [garden](/wiki/Garden "Garden")", in which "the relatively extensive, well-documented (both pictorially and written) encounters [...] between [them] provided a very early opportunity for meetings and mutual observation".[[155]](#cite_note-160)

The longest-running branch of the [Royal Society](/wiki/Royal_Society "Royal Society") outside of the United Kingdom is the [Royal Society of Tasmania](/wiki/Royal_Society_of_Tasmania "Royal Society of Tasmania") which was summoned in 1843. The Tasmanian Society of Natural History had been formed previously in 1838 before its merger with the Royal Society in 1849. It had been served by early [botanists](/wiki/Botany "Botany") working in Tasmania such as [Ronald Gunn](/wiki/Ronald_Campbell_Gunn "Ronald Campbell Gunn") and his correspondences.[[156]](#cite_note-161)[[157]](#cite_note-162)

Although [Tamworth](/wiki/Tamworth,_New_South_Wales "Tamworth, New South Wales") in [New South Wales](/wiki/New_South_Wales "New South Wales") is often credited[[158]](#cite_note-163) as being the first place in Australia with [electric](/wiki/Electricity "Electricity") [street lighting](/wiki/Street_light "Street light") in 1888, [Waratah](/wiki/Waratah,_Tasmania "Waratah, Tasmania") in [North West Tasmania](/wiki/North_West_Tasmania "North West Tasmania") was actually the first place to do so in Australia in 1886, although at a smaller scale.[[159]](#cite_note-164)

## Culture

### Literature

Main article: [Tasmanian literature](/wiki/Tasmanian_literature "Tasmanian literature")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/f3/RichardFlanagan_300w.jpg/250px-RichardFlanagan_300w.jpg)](/wiki/File:RichardFlanagan_300w.jpg)

[Man Booker Prize](/wiki/Man_Booker_Prize "Man Booker Prize")-winner [Richard Flanagan](/wiki/Richard_Flanagan "Richard Flanagan") has written several novels set in his home state of Tasmania.

Notable titles by Tasmanian authors include *[The Museum of Modern Love](/wiki/The_Museum_of_Modern_Love "The Museum of Modern Love")*[[160]](#cite_note-165)[[161]](#cite_note-166) by [Heather Rose](/wiki/Heather_Rose "Heather Rose"), *The Narrow Road to the Deep North* by [Richard Flanagan](/wiki/Richard_Flanagan "Richard Flanagan"), *The Alphabet of Light and Dark* by [Danielle Wood](/wiki/Danielle_Wood_(writer) "Danielle Wood (writer)"), *[The Roving Party](/wiki/The_Roving_Party "The Roving Party")* by Rohan Wilson and *[The Year of Living Dangerously](/wiki/The_Year_of_Living_Dangerously_(novel) "The Year of Living Dangerously (novel)")* by [Christopher Koch](/wiki/Christopher_Koch "Christopher Koch"), *The Rain Queen*[[162]](#cite_note-167) by [Katherine Scholes](/wiki/Katherine_Scholes "Katherine Scholes"), *Bridget Crack*[[163]](#cite_note-168) by Rachel Leary, and *The Blue Day Book* by [Bradley Trevor Greive](/wiki/Bradley_Trevor_Greive "Bradley Trevor Greive"). A small part of [Helen Garner](/wiki/Helen_Garner "Helen Garner")'s *[Monkey Grip](/wiki/Monkey_Grip_(novel) "Monkey Grip (novel)")* is set in Hobart as the main characters take a sojourn there. Children's books include *They Found a Cave* by [Nan Chauncy](/wiki/Nan_Chauncy "Nan Chauncy"), *The Museum of Thieves* by [Lian Tanner](/wiki/Lian_Tanner "Lian Tanner"), *Finding Serendipity*, *A Week Without Tuesday* and *Blueberry Pancakes Forever*[[164]](#cite_note-169) by Angelica Banks, *[Tiger Tale](/wiki/Tiger_Tale "Tiger Tale")* by Marion and Steve Isham. Tasmania is home to the eminent literary magazine that was formed in 1979, [Island magazine](/wiki/Island_magazine "Island magazine"), and the biennial Tasmanian Writers and Readers Festival, now renamed the Hobart Writers Festival.

[Tasmanian Gothic](/wiki/Tasmanian_Gothic "Tasmanian Gothic") is a literary genre which expresses the island state's "peculiar 'otherness' in relation to the mainland, as a remote, mysterious and self-enclosed place."[[165]](#cite_note-170) [Marcus Clarke](/wiki/Marcus_Clarke "Marcus Clarke")'s novel *[For the Term of his Natural Life](/wiki/For_the_Term_of_his_Natural_Life "For the Term of his Natural Life")*, written in the 1870s and set in convict era Tasmania, is a seminal example. This distinctive [Gothic](/wiki/Gothic_fiction "Gothic fiction") is not just restricted to literature, but can be represented through all [the arts](/wiki/The_arts "The arts"), such as in [painting](/wiki/Painting "Painting"), [music](/wiki/Music "Music"), or [architecture](/wiki/Architecture "Architecture").

### Visual arts

The biennial *Tasmanian Living Artists' Week* is a ten-day statewide festival for Tasmania's visual artists. The fourth festival in 2007 involved more than 1000 artists. Tasmania is home to two winners of the prestigious [Archibald Prize](/wiki/Archibald_Prize "Archibald Prize")—[Jack Carington Smith](/wiki/Jack_Carington_Smith "Jack Carington Smith") in 1963 for a portrait of [James McAuley](/wiki/James_McAuley "James McAuley"), and [Geoffrey Dyer](/wiki/Geoffrey_Dyer "Geoffrey Dyer") in 2003 for his portrait of [Richard Flanagan](/wiki/Richard_Flanagan "Richard Flanagan"). Photographers [Olegas Truchanas](/wiki/Olegas_Truchanas "Olegas Truchanas") and [Peter Dombrovskis](/wiki/Peter_Dombrovskis "Peter Dombrovskis") are known for works that became iconic in the [Lake Pedder](/wiki/Lake_Pedder "Lake Pedder") and [Franklin Dam](/wiki/Franklin_Dam "Franklin Dam") conservation movements. English-born painter [John Glover](/wiki/John_Glover_(artist) "John Glover (artist)") (1767–1849) is known for his paintings of Tasmanian landscapes, and is the namesake for the annual [Glover Prize](/wiki/Glover_Prize "Glover Prize"), which is awarded to the best landscape painting of Tasmania. The [Museum of Old and New Art](/wiki/Museum_of_Old_and_New_Art "Museum of Old and New Art") (MONA) opened in January 2011 at the [Moorilla Estate](/wiki/Moorilla_Estate "Moorilla Estate") in [Berriedale](/wiki/Berriedale,_Tasmania "Berriedale, Tasmania"),[[166]](#cite_note-171) and is the largest privately owned museum complex in Australia.[[167]](#cite_note-172)

The [Museum of Old and New Art](/wiki/Museum_of_Old_and_New_Art "Museum of Old and New Art") (MONA), the largest privately owned museum in the Southern Hemisphere

### Music and performing arts

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/a0/Princess_Theatre_at_night%2C_Launceston.JPG/250px-Princess_Theatre_at_night%2C_Launceston.JPG)](/wiki/File:Princess_Theatre_at_night,_Launceston.JPG)

The [Princess Theatre](/wiki/Princess_Theatre,_Launceston "Princess Theatre, Launceston") and Earl Arts Centre, [Launceston](/wiki/Launceston,_Tasmania "Launceston, Tasmania")

Tasmania has a varied musical scene, ranging from the [Tasmanian Symphony Orchestra](/wiki/Tasmanian_Symphony_Orchestra "Tasmanian Symphony Orchestra") whose home is the [Federation Concert Hall](/wiki/Hotel_Grand_Chancellor,_Hobart "Hotel Grand Chancellor, Hobart"), to a substantial number of small bands, orchestras, string quintets, saxophone ensembles and individual artists who perform at a variety of venues around the state. Tasmania is also home to a vibrant community of composers including [Constantine Koukias](/wiki/Constantine_Koukias "Constantine Koukias"), [Maria Grenfell](/wiki/Maria_Grenfell "Maria Grenfell") and [Don Kay](/wiki/Don_Kay_(composer) "Don Kay (composer)"). Tasmania is also home to one of Australia's leading new music institutions, [IHOS Music Theatre and Opera](/wiki/IHOS_Music_Theatre_and_Opera "IHOS Music Theatre and Opera") and gospel choirs, the [Southern Gospel Choir](/wiki/Southern_Gospel_Choir "Southern Gospel Choir"). Prominent Australian metal bands [Psycroptic](/wiki/Psycroptic "Psycroptic") and [Striborg](/wiki/Striborg "Striborg") hail from Tasmania.[[168]](#cite_note-173) Noir-rock band [The Paradise Motel](/wiki/The_Paradise_Motel "The Paradise Motel") and 1980s power-pop band [The Innocents](/wiki/The_Innocents_(Australian_band) "The Innocents (Australian band)")[[169]](#cite_note-174) are also citizens.

The [Tasmanian Aboriginals](/wiki/Tasmanian_Aboriginals "Tasmanian Aboriginals") were known to have sung [oral traditions](/wiki/Oral_traditions "Oral traditions"), as [Fanny Cochrane Smith](/wiki/Fanny_Cochrane_Smith "Fanny Cochrane Smith") (the last fluent speaker of any [Tasmanian language](/wiki/Tasmanian_languages "Tasmanian languages")) had done so in recordings from 1899 to 1903.[[170]](#cite_note-175)[[171]](#cite_note-176) Tasmania has been home to some early and prominent [Australian composers](/wiki/List_of_Australian_composers "List of Australian composers"). In piano, [Kitty Parker](/wiki/Katharine_Parker "Katharine Parker") from [Longford](/wiki/Longford,_Tasmania "Longford, Tasmania") was described by world-famous Australian composer [Percy Grainger](/wiki/Percy_Grainger "Percy Grainger") as his most gifted student.[[172]](#cite_note-177) [Peter Sculthorpe](/wiki/Peter_Sculthorpe "Peter Sculthorpe") was originally from [Launceston](/wiki/Launceston,_Tasmania "Launceston, Tasmania") and became well known in Australia for his works which were influenced by his Tasmanian origins, and he is, by coincidence, distantly related to Fanny Cochrane Smith.[[173]](#cite_note-178) In 1996, Sculthorpe composed the piece *Port Arthur: In Memoriam* for chamber [orchestra](/wiki/Orchestra "Orchestra"), which was first performed by the [Tasmanian Symphony Orchestra](/wiki/Tasmanian_Symphony_Orchestra "Tasmanian Symphony Orchestra").[[174]](#cite_note-179) [Charles Sandys Packer](/wiki/Charles_Sandys_Packer "Charles Sandys Packer") was an early Tasmanian example of the tradition of [Australian classical music](/wiki/Australian_classical_music "Australian classical music"), transported for the crime of embezzlement in 1839, and at a similar time [Francis Hartwell Henslowe](/wiki/Francis_Hartwell_Henslowe "Francis Hartwell Henslowe") had spent time as a [public servant](/wiki/Civil_servant "Civil servant") in Tasmania. [Amy Sherwin](/wiki/Amy_Sherwin "Amy Sherwin"), known as the *Tasmanian Nightingale* was a successful [soprano](/wiki/Soprano "Soprano"),[[175]](#cite_note-180) and [Eileen Joyce](/wiki/Eileen_Joyce "Eileen Joyce"), who came from remote [Zeehan](/wiki/Zeehan "Zeehan"), became a world-renowned pianist at the time of her peak.[[176]](#cite_note-181)

Further information: [Tasmanian Bands League](/wiki/Tasmanian_Bands_League "Tasmanian Bands League")

### Cinema

See also: [List of films shot in Tasmania](/wiki/List_of_films_shot_in_Tasmania "List of films shot in Tasmania") and [Category:Television shows set in Tasmania](/wiki/Category:Television_shows_set_in_Tasmania "Category:Television shows set in Tasmania")

Films set in Tasmania include *[Young Einstein](/wiki/Young_Einstein "Young Einstein")*, *[The Tale of Ruby Rose](/wiki/The_Tale_of_Ruby_Rose "The Tale of Ruby Rose")*, *[The Hunter](/wiki/The_Hunter_(2011_Australian_film) "The Hunter (2011 Australian film)")*, *[The Last Confession of Alexander Pearce](/wiki/The_Last_Confession_of_Alexander_Pearce "The Last Confession of Alexander Pearce")*, *Arctic Blast*, *[Manganinnie](/wiki/Manganinnie "Manganinnie")* (with music composed by [Peter Sculthorpe](/wiki/Peter_Sculthorpe "Peter Sculthorpe")), *[Van Diemen's Land](/wiki/Van_Diemen%27s_Land_(film) "Van Diemen's Land (film)")*, *[Lion](/wiki/Lion_(2016_film) "Lion (2016 film)")*, and *[The Nightingale](/wiki/The_Nightingale_(2018_film) "The Nightingale (2018 film)")*. Common within [Australian cinema](/wiki/Australian_cinema "Australian cinema"), the Tasmanian landscape is a focal point in most of their feature film productions. *The Last Confession of Alexander Pearce* and *Van Diemen's Land* are both set during an episode of Tasmania's convict history. Tasmanian film production goes as far back as the [silent era](/wiki/Silent_era "Silent era"), with the epic *[For The Term of His Natural Life](/wiki/For_the_Term_of_His_Natural_Life_(1927_film) "For the Term of His Natural Life (1927 film)")* in 1927 being the most expensive feature film made on Australian shores. *[The Kettering Incident](/wiki/The_Kettering_Incident "The Kettering Incident")*, filmed in and around [Kettering, Tasmania](/wiki/Kettering,_Tasmania "Kettering, Tasmania"), won the 2016 AACTA Award for Best Telefeature or Miniseries. The [documentary series](/wiki/Nature_documentary "Nature documentary") *[Walking with Dinosaurs](/wiki/Walking_with_Dinosaurs "Walking with Dinosaurs")* was partly filmed in Tasmania due to its terrain.

The [Tasmanian Film Corporation](/wiki/Tasmanian_Film_Corporation "Tasmanian Film Corporation"), which financed *[Manganinnie](/wiki/Manganinnie "Manganinnie")*, was the successor to the Tasmanian Government Department of Film Production but disappeared after privatisation. Its role is now filled by the [Australian Broadcasting Corporation](/wiki/Australian_Broadcasting_Corporation "Australian Broadcasting Corporation"), Screen Tasmania, and private ventures such as Blue Rocket Productions.

### Higher education

Tasmania is served by the [University of Tasmania](/wiki/University_of_Tasmania "University of Tasmania"), a research university established in 1846.

### Media

See also: [Tasmanian media](/wiki/Tasmanian_media "Tasmanian media")

Tasmania has five broadcast television stations which produce local content including [ABC Tasmania](/wiki/ABC_Tasmania "ABC Tasmania"), [Seven Tasmania](/wiki/TNT_(Australian_TV_station) "TNT (Australian TV station)"), [WIN Television Tasmania](/wiki/TVT_(TV_station) "TVT (TV station)") – an affiliate of the [Nine Network](/wiki/Nine_Network "Nine Network"), [10 Tasmania](/wiki/TDT_(TV_station) "TDT (TV station)") – an affiliate of [Network 10](/wiki/Network_10 "Network 10") (joint owned by WIN and [Southern Cross](/wiki/Southern_Cross_Media_Group "Southern Cross Media Group")), and [SBS](/wiki/Special_Broadcasting_Service "Special Broadcasting Service").

### Sport

Main article: [Sport in Tasmania](/wiki/Sport_in_Tasmania "Sport in Tasmania")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/59/Bellerive_oval_hobart.jpg/250px-Bellerive_oval_hobart.jpg)](/wiki/File:Bellerive_oval_hobart.jpg)

[Bellerive Oval](/wiki/Bellerive_Oval "Bellerive Oval") hosts [cricket](/wiki/Cricket "Cricket") and [Australian rules football](/wiki/Australian_rules_football "Australian rules football"), Tasmania's two most popular spectator sports.

Sport is an important pastime in Tasmania, and the state has produced several famous sportsmen and women and also hosted several major sporting events. The [Tasmanian Tigers](/wiki/Tasmanian_Tigers "Tasmanian Tigers") cricket team represents the state successfully (for example the [Sheffield Shield](/wiki/Sheffield_Shield "Sheffield Shield") in 2007, 2011 and 2013) and plays its home games at the [Bellerive Oval](/wiki/Bellerive_Oval "Bellerive Oval") in Hobart, which is also the home ground for the [Hobart Hurricanes](/wiki/Hobart_Hurricanes "Hobart Hurricanes") in the Big Bash League. In addition, Bellerive Oval regularly hosts international cricket matches. Famous Tasmanian cricketers include [David Boon](/wiki/David_Boon "David Boon"), former Australian captains [Ricky Ponting](/wiki/Ricky_Ponting "Ricky Ponting") and [Tim Paine](/wiki/Tim_Paine "Tim Paine").

[Australian rules football in Tasmania](/wiki/Australian_rules_football_in_Tasmania "Australian rules football in Tasmania") is the most watched form of football and a [Tasmanian team](/wiki/Tasmanian_AFL_Bid "Tasmanian AFL Bid") was awarded a license to enter the [Australian Football League](/wiki/Australian_Football_League "Australian Football League") (AFL) in 2028 to be based out of a new [Macquarie Point Stadium](/wiki/Macquarie_Point_Stadium "Macquarie Point Stadium"). AFL matches have been played since 2001 at [Aurora Stadium](/wiki/York_Park "York Park") in Launceston and Bellerive Oval in Hobart. Local leagues include the [North West Football League](/wiki/North_West_Football_League "North West Football League") and [Tasmanian State League](/wiki/Tasmanian_State_League "Tasmanian State League").

[Soccer in Tasmania](/wiki/Soccer_in_Tasmania "Soccer in Tasmania") is the most participated football code and there is an active [Tasmanian A-League bid](/wiki/Tasmanian_A-League_bid "Tasmanian A-League bid"). The existing statewide league is the [NPL Tasmania](/wiki/National_Premier_Leagues_Tasmania "National Premier Leagues Tasmania").

[Rugby Union](/wiki/Rugby_Union "Rugby Union") is also played in Tasmania and is governed by the [Tasmanian Rugby Union](/wiki/Tasmanian_Rugby_Union "Tasmanian Rugby Union"). Ten clubs take part in the statewide Tasmanian Rugby Competition.

Tasmania hosts the professional [Moorilla International](/wiki/Moorilla_Hobart_International "Moorilla Hobart International") tennis tournament as part of the lead up to the Australian Open and is played at the [Hobart International Tennis Centre](/wiki/Hobart_International_Tennis_Centre "Hobart International Tennis Centre"), Hobart.

The [Sydney to Hobart Yacht Race](/wiki/Sydney_to_Hobart_Yacht_Race "Sydney to Hobart Yacht Race") is an annual event starting in Sydney, NSW, on Boxing Day and finishing in Hobart, Tasmania. It is widely considered to be one of the most difficult yacht races in the world.[[177]](#cite_note-182)

In basketball, Tasmania has previously been represented in the [National Basketball League (NBL)](/wiki/National_Basketball_League_(Australia) "National Basketball League (Australia)") by [Launceston Casino City](/wiki/Launceston_Casino_City "Launceston Casino City") (1980–1982), the [Devonport Warriors](/wiki/Devonport_Warriors "Devonport Warriors") (1983–1984), and the [Hobart Devils](/wiki/Hobart_Devils "Hobart Devils") (1983–1996). Since the [2021–22 NBL season](/wiki/2021%E2%80%9322_NBL_season "2021–22 NBL season"), Tasmania has been represented by the [Tasmania JackJumpers](/wiki/Tasmania_JackJumpers "Tasmania JackJumpers"), a state-wide franchise which plays its home games in both Hobart and Launceston. The JackJumpers secured their maiden [NBL championship](/wiki/List_of_NBL_champions "List of NBL champions") in the [2023–24 season](/wiki/2023%E2%80%9324_NBL_season "2023–24 NBL season"), marking Tasmania's first NBL title since Launceston Casino City in 1981.[[178]](#cite_note-183)

### Cuisine

See also: [Tasmanian wine](/wiki/Tasmanian_wine "Tasmanian wine")

Tasmanian Aboriginal people had a diverse diet, including native currants, pigface, and native plums, and a wide range of birds and kangaroos. Seafood has always been a significant part of the Tasmanian diet, including its wide range of shellfish, which are still commercially farmed[[179]](#cite_note-goway-184) such as [crayfish](/wiki/Crayfish "Crayfish"), [orange roughy](/wiki/Orange_roughy "Orange roughy"), [salmon](/wiki/Salmon_(food) "Salmon (food)")[[179]](#cite_note-goway-184) and [oysters](/wiki/Oyster "Oyster").[[179]](#cite_note-goway-184) Seal meat also formed a significant part of the Aboriginal diet.[[180]](#cite_note-185)

Tasmania's non-Aboriginal cuisine has a unique history from that of mainland Australia. It has developed through many subsequent waves of immigration. Tasmanian traditional foods include scallop pies – a pie filled with scallops in curry – and curry powder, which was popularised by [Keen's Curry](/wiki/Keen%27s "Keen's") in the 19th century.[[181]](#cite_note-186) Tasmania also produces and consumes wasabi, saffron, truffles and leatherwood honey.[[182]](#cite_note-187)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/64/Cascade_Brewery_1.jpg/250px-Cascade_Brewery_1.jpg)](/wiki/File:Cascade_Brewery_1.jpg)

Built in Hobart in 1824, [Cascade Brewery](/wiki/Cascade_Brewery "Cascade Brewery") is Australia's oldest continuously operating brewery.

Tasmania now has a wide range of restaurants, in part due to the arrival of immigrants and changing cultural patterns. Scattered across Tasmania are many vineyards,[[179]](#cite_note-goway-184) and Tasmanian beer brands such as [Boags](/wiki/Boags "Boags") and [Cascade](/wiki/Cascade_Brewery "Cascade Brewery") are known and sold in mainland Australia. [King Island](/wiki/King_Island_(Tasmania) "King Island (Tasmania)") off the northwestern coast of Tasmania has a reputation for boutique cheeses[[179]](#cite_note-goway-184) and dairy products.

The *Central Cookery Book* was written in 1930 by [A. C. Irvine](/wiki/Alice_Christina_Irvine "Alice Christina Irvine") and is still popular in Australia and even internationally.[[183]](#cite_note-188)[[184]](#cite_note-189) Tasmanian cuisine is often unique and has won many awards. One example is the Hartshorn Distillery, which has won prizes in the World [Vodka](/wiki/Vodka "Vodka") Awards for three years in a row since 2017.[[185]](#cite_note-190)

### Events

Main article: [List of events in Tasmania](/wiki/List_of_events_in_Tasmania "List of events in Tasmania")

To foster tourism, the state government encourages or supports several annual events in and around the island. The best known of these is the [Sydney to Hobart Yacht Race](/wiki/Sydney_to_Hobart_Yacht_Race "Sydney to Hobart Yacht Race"), starting on [Boxing Day](/wiki/Boxing_Day "Boxing Day") in Sydney and usually arriving at [Constitution Dock](/wiki/Constitution_Dock "Constitution Dock") in Hobart around three to four days later, during the [Taste of Tasmania](/wiki/Taste_of_Tasmania "Taste of Tasmania"), an annual food and wine festival. Other events include the [Targa Tasmania](/wiki/Targa_Tasmania "Targa Tasmania") [rally](/wiki/Rallying "Rallying") which attracts rally drivers from around the world and is staged all over the state, over five days. Rural or regional events include [Agfest](/wiki/Agfest "Agfest"), a three-day [agricultural show](/wiki/Agricultural_show "Agricultural show") held at [Carrick](/wiki/Carrick,_Tasmania "Carrick, Tasmania") (just west of Launceston) in early May and [NASA](/wiki/NASA "NASA") supported TastroFest – Tasmania's Astronomy Festival, held early August in [Ulverstone](/wiki/Ulverstone,_Tasmania "Ulverstone, Tasmania") (Northwest Tasmania). The [Royal Hobart Show](/wiki/Royal_Hobart_Show "Royal Hobart Show") and [Royal Launceston Show](/wiki/Royal_Launceston_Show "Royal Launceston Show") are both held in October annually.

Music events held in Tasmania include the [Falls Festival](/wiki/Falls_Festival "Falls Festival") at [Marion Bay](/wiki/Marion_Bay,_Tasmania "Marion Bay, Tasmania") (a [Victorian](/wiki/Victoria_(Australia) "Victoria (Australia)") event now held in both Victoria and Tasmania on New Year's Eve); the Festival of Voices, a national celebration of song held each year in Hobart attracting international and national teachers and choirs in the heart of Winter; and [MS Fest](/wiki/MS_Fest "MS Fest"), a charity music event held in Launceston to raise money for those with [multiple sclerosis](/wiki/Multiple_sclerosis "Multiple sclerosis"). The [Cygnet Folk Festival](/wiki/Cygnet_Folk_Festival "Cygnet Folk Festival") is one of Australia's most iconic [folk music](/wiki/Folk_music "Folk music") festivals and is held in [Cygnet](/wiki/Cygnet,_Tasmania "Cygnet, Tasmania") in the [Huon Valley](/wiki/Huon_Valley "Huon Valley") every year in January.[[186]](#cite_note-191) The Tasmanian Lute Festival is an early music event held in different locations in Tasmania every two years. Recent additions to the state arts events calendar include the [10 Days on the Island](/wiki/10_Days_on_the_Island "10 Days on the Island") arts festival, [MONA FOMA](/wiki/MONA_FOMA "MONA FOMA"), run by [David Walsh](/wiki/David_Walsh_(art_collector) "David Walsh (art collector)") and curated by [Brian Ritchie](/wiki/Brian_Ritchie "Brian Ritchie") and [Dark Mofo](/wiki/Dark_Mofo "Dark Mofo") also run by [David Walsh](/wiki/David_Walsh_(art_collector) "David Walsh (art collector)") and curated by Leigh Carmichael.

[The Unconformity](/wiki/The_Unconformity "The Unconformity") is a three-day festival held every two years in [Queenstown](/wiki/Queenstown,_Tasmania "Queenstown, Tasmania") on the [West Coast](/wiki/West_Coast_of_Tasmania "West Coast of Tasmania").[[187]](#cite_note-192)[[188]](#cite_note-193) Each February in [Evandale](/wiki/Evandale,_Tasmania "Evandale, Tasmania") a [penny-farthing](/wiki/Penny-farthing "Penny-farthing") championships are held.[[189]](#cite_note-194)

### Perception within Australia

Tasmania is perceived within Australia and internationally as an island with pristine wildlife, water and air. It is known for its [ecotourism](/wiki/Ecotourism "Ecotourism") for these reasons, and is considered an idyllic location for Australians considering a "tree-" or "sea-change", or are seeking [retirement](/wiki/Retirement "Retirement") because of Tasmania's [temperate environment](/wiki/Temperate_climate "Temperate climate") and friendly locals.[[190]](#cite_note-195) In other parts of the world, Tasmania is considered as the opposite side of the planet to most places, and supposedly home to mythically exotic animals, such as the [Tasmanian Devil](/wiki/Tasmanian_Devil_(Looney_Tunes) "Tasmanian Devil (Looney Tunes)") as popularised by [Warner Brothers](/wiki/Warner_Brothers "Warner Brothers").

#### Stereotypes

Tasmania has a reputation within Australia that is often at odds with the reality of the state or may have only been true during colonial times and has only persevered on the Australian mainland as a [myth](/wiki/Myth "Myth"). Because of these [stereotypes](/wiki/Stereotypes "Stereotypes"), Tasmania is often referred to as the primary target (i.e., "butt") of mainland Australian jokes.[[191]](#cite_note-196) In more recent times, references to insults against Tasmania are more [sarcastic](/wiki/Sarcasm "Sarcasm") and jovial, but angst against the island still exists. The most commonly cited sarcastic comment is on the supposedly '[two-headed](/wiki/Polycephaly "Polycephaly")' Tasmanians, which originated due to some colonists developing [goitres](/wiki/Goitre "Goitre") from the low amount of [iodine](/wiki/Iodine "Iodine") in the island's soil.[[192]](#cite_note-197) But as Tasmania receives higher volumes of inter-state tourists, the perceptions are in the process of changing.[[193]](#cite_note-198)

The most prominent example of negative stereotype is of [inbreeding](/wiki/Inbreeding "Inbreeding") due to the relatively small size of Tasmania compared to the rest of Australia (though Tasmania is nearly as large as the Republic of Ireland in area, and more populous than [Iceland](/wiki/Iceland "Iceland")). This is untrue and if it had once been the case, it would have existed in the rest of colonial Australia as well, though Tasmania's penal establishments were some of the harshest in the entire colony and home to infamous bushrangers. This is a part of the also-receding global stereotype that all Australians are or were derived from criminals, even as most [convicts](/wiki/Convicts_in_Australia "Convicts in Australia") were transported for petty crimes. During this period of European settlement, Tasmania was the second centre of power (and a significant port of the [British Empire](/wiki/British_Empire "British Empire")) on the continent after [New South Wales](/wiki/New_South_Wales "New South Wales"), before being surpassed in the latter half of the 19th century by [Victoria](/wiki/Victoria_(Australia) "Victoria (Australia)") and regions sustained by [mining booms](/wiki/Australian_gold_rushes "Australian gold rushes") following the cessation of transportation in 1853.[[194]](#cite_note-199)
A mentality developed in certain corners of Australia, and led to a general dislike of Tasmania amongst these people, even if the opinion-holder had never properly visited. It can rise to such an extent as to argue for the secession of Tasmania from the rest of Australia, in an effort to 'recover' Australia's reputation from Tasmania.[[195]](#cite_note-200)

## Transport

Further information: [Transport in Australia](/wiki/Transport_in_Australia "Transport in Australia")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/64/HobartAirportTerminal.jpg/250px-HobartAirportTerminal.jpg)](/wiki/File:HobartAirportTerminal.jpg)

[Hobart Airport](/wiki/Hobart_Airport "Hobart Airport")

### Air

Tasmania's main air carriers are [Jetstar](/wiki/Jetstar "Jetstar") and [Virgin Australia](/wiki/Virgin_Australia "Virgin Australia"); [Qantas](/wiki/Qantas "Qantas") and [QantasLink](/wiki/QantasLink "QantasLink"). These airlines fly direct routes to [Brisbane](/wiki/Brisbane_Airport "Brisbane Airport"), [Melbourne](/wiki/Melbourne_Airport "Melbourne Airport") and [Sydney](/wiki/Sydney_Airport "Sydney Airport"). Major airports include [Hobart Airport](/wiki/Hobart_Airport "Hobart Airport") and [Launceston Airport](/wiki/Launceston_Airport "Launceston Airport"); the smaller airports, [Burnie](/wiki/Burnie_Airport "Burnie Airport") (Wynyard) and [King Island](/wiki/King_Island_Airport "King Island Airport"), serviced by [Rex Airlines](/wiki/Rex_Airlines "Rex Airlines"); and [Devonport](/wiki/Devonport_Airport "Devonport Airport"), serviced by QantasLink; have services to Melbourne. Intra-Tasmanian air services are offered by [Par Avion](/wiki/Par_Avion_(airline) "Par Avion (airline)"). Until 2001 [Ansett Australia](/wiki/Ansett_Australia "Ansett Australia") operated majorly out of Tasmania to 12 destinations nationwide. Tourism-related air travel is also represented in Tasmania, such as in the [Par Avion](/wiki/Par_Avion_(airline) "Par Avion (airline)") route between [Cambridge Aerodrome](/wiki/Cambridge_Aerodrome "Cambridge Aerodrome") near Hobart to [Melaleuca](/wiki/Melaleuca,_Tasmania "Melaleuca, Tasmania") in [Southwest National Park](/wiki/Southwest_National_Park "Southwest National Park").

### Antarctica base

Tasmania – Hobart in particular – serves as Australia's chief sea link to Antarctica, with the [Australian Antarctic Division](/wiki/Australian_Antarctic_Division "Australian Antarctic Division") located in [Kingston](/wiki/Kingston,_Tasmania "Kingston, Tasmania"). Hobart is also the home port of the French ship *l'Astrolabe*, which makes regular supply runs to the [French Southern Territories](/wiki/French_Southern_and_Antarctic_Lands "French Southern and Antarctic Lands") near and in Antarctica.

### Road

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/16/Bridgewater_Bridge%2C_Tasmania.jpg/250px-Bridgewater_Bridge%2C_Tasmania.jpg)](/wiki/File:Bridgewater_Bridge,_Tasmania.jpg)

Former [Bridgewater Bridge](/wiki/Bridgewater_Bridge "Bridgewater Bridge")

Within the state, the primary form of transport is by road. Since the 1980s, many of the [state's highways](/wiki/List_of_highways_in_Tasmania "List of highways in Tasmania") have undergone regular upgrades. These include the Hobart [Southern Outlet](/wiki/Southern_Outlet,_Hobart "Southern Outlet, Hobart"), Launceston Southern Outlet, [Bass Highway](/wiki/Bass_Highway_(Tasmania) "Bass Highway (Tasmania)") reconstruction, and the [Huon Highway](/wiki/Huon_Highway "Huon Highway"). Public transport is provided by [Metro Tasmania](/wiki/Metro_Tasmania "Metro Tasmania") bus services, regular taxis and Hobart only[[196]](#cite_note-201) UBER ride-share services within urban areas, with [Kinetic Tasmania](/wiki/Kinetic_Tasmania "Kinetic Tasmania"), [Tassielink Transit](/wiki/Tassielink_Transit "Tassielink Transit"), Manions Coaches, Area Connect and Calows Coaches providing bus service between population centres.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/9f/West_Coast_Wilderness_Railway_%284754055363%29.jpg/250px-West_Coast_Wilderness_Railway_%284754055363%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:West_Coast_Wilderness_Railway_(4754055363).jpg)

The [West Coast Wilderness Railway](/wiki/West_Coast_Wilderness_Railway "West Coast Wilderness Railway") runs from [Queenstown](/wiki/Queenstown,_Tasmania "Queenstown, Tasmania") to [Strahan](/wiki/Strahan,_Tasmania "Strahan, Tasmania") and is a reminder of the once elaborate network of rail in Tasmania.

### Rail

Main article: [Rail transport in Tasmania](/wiki/Rail_transport_in_Tasmania "Rail transport in Tasmania")

[Rail transport in Tasmania](/wiki/Rail_transport_in_Tasmania "Rail transport in Tasmania") consists of narrow-gauge lines to major population centres and to mining and forestry operations on the west coast and in the northwest. Services are operated by [TasRail](/wiki/TasRail "TasRail"). Regular [passenger train](/wiki/Passenger_train "Passenger train") services in the state ceased in 1978; the only scheduled trains are freight, but there are tourist and heritage trains on several closed alignments, for example the [West Coast Wilderness Railway](/wiki/West_Coast_Wilderness_Railway "West Coast Wilderness Railway") between [Queenstown](/wiki/Queenstown,_Tasmania "Queenstown, Tasmania") and [Strahan](/wiki/Strahan,_Tasmania "Strahan, Tasmania"). There is an [ongoing proposal](/wiki/Riverline_(Tasmania) "Riverline (Tasmania)") to reinstate commuter trains to Hobart. This idea however lacks political motivation due to ongoing scrutiny about the [Macquarie Point Stadium](/wiki/Macquarie_Point_Stadium "Macquarie Point Stadium"). The centre of TasRail's freight network is the state of the art [Brighton Transport Hub](/wiki/Brighton_Transport_Hub "Brighton Transport Hub") in Hobart's northern suburbs. Other major rail hubs are located at [Burnie](/wiki/Burnie "Burnie") and [East Tamar Junction](/w/index.php?title=East_Tamar_Junction&action=edit&redlink=1 "East Tamar Junction (page does not exist)"). Rail maintenance and loading facilities are located here. The busiest rail service in the state is TasRail's [cement](/wiki/Cement "Cement") trains running between [Devonport](/wiki/Devonport,_Tasmania "Devonport, Tasmania") and [Railton](/wiki/Railton,_Tasmania "Railton, Tasmania"). Mainline heritage rail services ceased in 2004 after [Pacific National](/wiki/Pacific_National "Pacific National") revoked their permission to run.

### Shipping

See also: [Bass Strait ferries](/wiki/Bass_Strait_ferries "Bass Strait ferries")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/51/Spirit_of_Tasmania_Port_Melbourne.jpg/250px-Spirit_of_Tasmania_Port_Melbourne.jpg)](/wiki/File:Spirit_of_Tasmania_Port_Melbourne.jpg)

The *[Spirit of Tasmania](/wiki/Spirit_of_Tasmania "Spirit of Tasmania")* links the island with mainland Australia.

There is a substantial amount of commercial and recreational shipping within Hobart's harbour, and the port hosts approximately 120 cruise ships during the warmer half of the year, and there are occasional visits from military vessels.[[197]](#cite_note-dpacmr-202)

Burnie and Devonport on the northwest coast host ports and several other coastal towns host either small fishing ports or substantial marinas. The domestic sea route between Tasmania and the mainland is serviced by [Bass Strait](/wiki/Bass_Strait "Bass Strait") passenger/vehicle ferries operated by the Tasmanian government-owned *[Spirit of Tasmania](/wiki/Spirit_of_Tasmania "Spirit of Tasmania")*. The state is also home to [Incat](/wiki/Incat "Incat"), a manufacturer of very high-speed aluminium catamarans that regularly broke records when they were first launched.
